arXiv:2605.06556v1 [math.PR] 07 May 2026

Probability of Quota Violations in Divisor Apportionment Methods with Nonzero Allocations

Tyler C. Wunder and Joseph W. Cutrone
Abstract

Apportionment assigns indivisible items among groups. By the Balinski-Young theorem, no method can satisfy both house monotonicity and the quota rule. This paper investigates quota violations caused by nonzero allocation constraints, and derives exact probability formulas for their frequency. Such violations occur in systems like the U.S. House of Representatives, where each state is guaranteed at least one seat. We analyze the three-state case, introduce the τ\tau statistic to parametrize population distributions, and prove an Asymptotic Quota Stabilization theorem: for fixed τ\tau, quota behavior stabilizes as populations grow, yielding probability results for quota violations determined by the set of ultimately violatory τ\tau values.

Applying this framework to the five classical divisor methods, we derive exact probability formulas. Additionally, we show that as the number of seats M→∞M\to\infty, these probabilities converge to method-specific constants. These results provide a precise, quantitative foundation for evaluating the fairness and frequency of quota violations in constrained apportionment systems.

1 Introduction and Foundations

1.1 Background on Apportionment

Let nn be the number of states with populations pi∈ℝ>0p_{i}\in\mathbb{R}_{>0} for 1≤i≤n1\leq i\leq n. To avoid ties, assume no two states have the same populations. Let M≥nM\geq n be the number of seats to apportion.

A divisor method is an algorithmic procedure to distribute the MM seats by dividing populations by a strictly increasing divisor function, δ:ℝ≥0→ℝ≥0\delta:\mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}\to\mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}.

Given populations (p1,…,pn)(p_{1},\dots,p_{n}) and a divisor function δ\delta, an apportionment A​(p1,…,pn)=(a1,…,an)A(p_{1},\dots,p_{n})=(a_{1},\dots,a_{n}), is calculated as follows:

  1. 1.

    Assign each state zero seats (or one seat if δ​(0)=0\delta(0)=0).

  2. 2.

    Calculate each state’s priority value piδ​(ri)\frac{p_{i}}{\delta(r_{i})}, where rir_{i} is the current number of seats assigned to state ii.

  3. 3.

    Assign to the state with the highest priority value an additional seat.

  4. 4.

    Repeat steps (2) and (3) until MM seats have been apportioned.

The five “workable methods” most commonly used are defined by the following divisor functions:

Method Divisor Function
Adams δ​(s)=s\delta(s)=s
Jefferson δ​(s)=s+1\delta(s)=s+1
Webster δ​(s)=s+0.5\delta(s)=s+0.5
Huntington-Hill δ​(s)=s​(s+1)\delta(s)=\sqrt{s(s+1)}
Dean δ​(s)=2​s​(s+1)2​s+1\delta(s)=\dfrac{2s(s+1)}{2s+1}

A state’s standard quota qiq_{i} is the number of seats proportional to its population. This generally non-integer number is defined by qi=piS​Dq_{i}=\frac{p_{i}}{SD}, where S​D=PMSD=\frac{P}{M} is the standard divisor, defined to be the total population P=∑i=1npiP=\sum_{i=1}^{n}p_{i} divided by the number of seats MM. Ideally, each state should be apportioned its standard quota. However, as the standard quota is rarely an integer, a state should instead receive its upper quota, ⌈qi⌉\lceil q_{i}\rceil, or its lower quota, ⌊qi⌋\lfloor q_{i}\rfloor. With this in mind, define the following notion of unfair apportionment:

A quota violation occurs whenever a state is assigned more seats than its upper quota or fewer seats than its lower quota. That is, ai>⌈qi⌉a_{i}>\lceil q_{i}\rceil or ai<⌊qi⌋a_{i}<\lfloor q_{i}\rfloor for some state ii. In particular, a lower quota violation occurs when ai<⌊qi⌋a_{i}<\lfloor q_{i}\rfloor, and an upper quota violation occurs when ai>⌈qi⌉a_{i}>\lceil q_{i}\rceil.

By the Balinski-Young Impossibility Theorem [2], all apportionment methods have either quota violations or other unfair occurrences called paradoxes. Specifically, the previously defined divisor methods are without paradoxes but all are known to violate quota.

1.2 Nonzero Allocation Constraints

In many applications, such as the apportionment of legislative representatives, it is undesirable for an apportionment method to assign zero representatives to any state. To ensure ai≥1a_{i}\geq 1, methods like Jefferson and Webster are modified such that δ​(0)=0\delta(0)=0. This modification can introduce additional quota violations not present in the unconstrained method where a small state receives a seat it would not have earned based purely on its priority value.

Definition 1.1.

For ordered populations p1,…,pnp_{1},\dots,p_{n}; MM seats; and a divisor method AA that guarantees nonzero allocations, define the modified method A~\tilde{A} as follows:

  1. 1.

    With M−1M-1 seats, calculate A​(p2,…,pn)A(p_{2},\dots,p_{n}) to obtain (a2,…,an)(a_{2},\dots,a_{n}).

  2. 2.

    Set a1=1a_{1}=1.

  3. 3.

    Define A~​(p1,…,pn)=(a1,…,an)\tilde{A}(p_{1},\dots,p_{n})=(a_{1},\dots,a_{n})

Note the order of the steps is important here. The modified method enforces a guaranteed seat for state one and applies the divisor method on the remaining M−1M-1 seats.

Definition 1.2.

Let AA be a divisor method that guarantees nonzero allocations on MM seats and suppose the populations are ordered such that p1<⋯<pnp_{1}<\cdots<p_{n}. The apportionment A​(p1,…,pn)A(p_{1},\dots,p_{n}) exhibits a quota violation caused by nonzero allocations if the following conditions hold:

  1. 1.

    A​(p1,…,pn)A(p_{1},\dots,p_{n}) violates the quota rule.

  2. 2.

    A~​(p1,…,pn)=A​(p1,…,pn)\tilde{A}(p_{1},\dots,p_{n})=A(p_{1},\dots,p_{n}).

  3. 3.

    When apportioning MM seats A​(p2,…,pn)A(p_{2},\dots,p_{n}) does not violate the quota rule.

Additionally, define a quota violation caused or worsened by nonzero allocations as when conditions 1 and 2 hold.

The motivation behind these definitions is to check if the smallest state has a priority value so low that they would not receive seats by having the largest priority value, and instead only receive a seat because the method guarantees each state at least one seat.

2 Main Results

The paper introduces a statistic τ\tau which is useful for studying quota violations caused by nonzero allocations in the three state case.

Definition 4.1.

Let (1,x,y)(1,x,y), 1<x<y1<x<y, be the associated reduced population vector for a three state distribution (p1,p2,p3p_{1},p_{2},p_{3}). Define the τ\tau statistic as follows:

τ:=mean​(1,x,y)−median​(1,x,y)max⁡(1,x,y).\tau:=\frac{\mathrm{mean}(1,x,y)-\mathrm{median}(1,x,y)}{\max(1,x,y)}.
Lemma 4.3.

The statistic τ\tau parametrizes a family of population distributions dependent on xx. Specifically, this is the set of reduced population vectors associated with a fixed τ\tau: {(1,x,y​(x,τ))∣x∈ℝ,x>1}\{(1,x,y(x,\tau))\mid x\in\mathbb{R},x>1\}, where yy is a linear function of xx.

Theorem 4.6.

(Asymptotic Apportionment) Let (1,x,y​(x,τ))(1,x,y(x,\tau)) be a reduced population vector associated with a fixed τ\tau, and let AA be a divisor method that guarantees nonzero allocations on MM seats. Then, for all but finitely many τ∈(−13,13)\tau\in(-\frac{1}{3},\frac{1}{3}):

limx→∞A​(1,x,y​(x,τ))=A~​(M​(1−3​τ)3−3​τ,2​M3−3​τ),\displaystyle{\lim_{x\to\infty}A(1,x,y(x,\tau))=\tilde{A}\left(\frac{M(1-3\tau)}{3-3\tau},\frac{2M}{3-3\tau}\right)},

where A~\tilde{A} is defined as apportioning the remaining M−1M-1 seats among states 2 and 3 according to the divisor method AA (see definition 1.1).

Theorem 4.7.

(Asymptotic Quota Stabilization for Nonexceptional τ\tau) Let ℰ\mathcal{E} be the exceptional set of finite τ∈(−13,13)\tau\in(-\frac{1}{3},\frac{1}{3}) such that Theorem 4.6 fails. Fix τ∉ℰ\tau\notin\mathcal{E}. Then there exists some xτx_{\tau} such that for all x≥xτx\geq x_{\tau}, A​(1,x,y​(x,τ))A(1,x,y(x,\tau)) is always a lower quota violation caused by nonzero allocation or never a violation, with this behavior determined by τ\tau.

Specifically, let state i∈{2,3}i\in\{2,3\} be the state that receives the last seat when apportioning MM seats among only states 22 and 33 from A​(q~2,q~3)A(\tilde{q}_{2},\tilde{q}_{3}). Then:

  1. 1.

    If A​(q~2,q~3)A(\tilde{q}_{2},\tilde{q}_{3}) assigns state ii its lower quota ⌊q~i⌋\lfloor\tilde{q}_{i}\rfloor, then for all x≥xτx\geq x_{\tau}, the apportionment A​(1,x,y​(x,τ))A(1,x,y(x,\tau)) is a lower quota violation caused by nonzero allocations, with state ii receiving ⌊q~i⌋−1\lfloor\tilde{q}_{i}\rfloor-1 seats.

  2. 2.

    If A​(q~2,q~3)A(\tilde{q}_{2},\tilde{q}_{3}) assigns state ii its upper quota ⌈q~i⌉\lceil\tilde{q}_{i}\rceil, then for all x≥xτx\geq x_{\tau}, the apportionment A​(1,x,y​(x,τ))A(1,x,y(x,\tau)) is not a quota violation.

Theorem 5.12.

Let AA be a divisor method with divisor function δ\delta that guarantees nonzero allocations on M seats and n=3n=3 states. Then, under the asymptotic uniform distribution on reduced population vectors (1,x,y)(1,x,y), with 1<x<y1<x<y, the probability of a quota violation is the same as the probability of a quota violation caused by states guaranteed nonzero allocations and is calculated by:

P​(A​(1,x,y)​ has a q.v.)=∑k=⌈M/2⌉M−1M​(1k−1k+D​(k))P(A(1,x,y)\text{ has a q.v.})=\sum_{k=\lceil M/2\rceil}^{M-1}M\left(\frac{1}{k}-\frac{1}{k+D(k)}\right)

where kk indexes the integer values of ⌊q~3⌋\lfloor\tilde{q}_{3}\rfloor, where

q~3=2​M3−3​τ,τ=mean​(1,x,y)−median​(1,x,y)max⁡(1,x,y)=1−2​x+y3​y.\tilde{q}_{3}=\frac{2M}{3-3\tau},\qquad\tau=\frac{\mathrm{mean}(1,x,y)-\mathrm{median}(1,x,y)}{\max(1,x,y)}=\dfrac{1-2x+y}{3y}.

and

D​(k)=(M−k)​δ​(k−1)−k​δ​(M−k−1)δ​(k−1)+δ​(M−k−1).D(k)=\frac{(M-k)\delta(k-1)-k\,\delta(M-k-1)}{\delta(k-1)+\delta(M-k-1)}.
Theorem 5.16.

Under the asymptotic uniform distribution for vectors (1,x,y)(1,x,y) with 1<x<y1<x<y, we have as M→∞M\to\infty that the probability of a quota violation caused by nonzero allocations converges to 2​ln⁡(2)−1≈0.3862922\ln(2)-1\approx 0.386292 in the Adams method, to 0 in the modified Jefferson method, and to ln⁡(2)−12≈0.193147\ln(2)-\frac{1}{2}\approx 0.193147 in the Huntington-Hill, Webster, and Dean methods.

Theorem 5.22.

Let MM be the total number of seats, and let AA be the Modified Jefferson, Modified Webster, Dean, or Huntington-Hill method with divisor function δ\delta. Let (1,x,y)(1,x,y) be a reduced population vector with 1<x<y1<x<y, drawn from a probability distribution with joint density f​(x,y)f(x,y).

For each τ∈(−13,13)\tau\in\left(-\frac{1}{3},\frac{1}{3}\right), the τ\tau-line parametrization in xx is

x=x​(y,τ):=12​y−32​τ​y+12,x=x(y,\tau):=\frac{1}{2}y-\frac{3}{2}\tau y+\frac{1}{2},

Let the set of ultimately violatory τ\tau values be

V=⋃k=⌊M/2⌋+1M−1(1−2​M3​k, 1−2​M3​(k+D​(k))),V=\bigcup_{k=\lfloor M/2\rfloor+1}^{M-1}\left(1-\frac{2M}{3k},\,1-\frac{2M}{3\bigl(k+D(k)\bigr)}\right),

where

D​(k)=(M−k)​δ​(k−1)−k​δ​(M−k−1)δ​(k−1)+δ​(M−k−1).D(k)=\frac{(M-k)\delta(k-1)-k\,\delta(M-k-1)}{\delta(k-1)+\delta(M-k-1)}.

For each τ\tau, let yτFy_{\tau}^{F} denote the floor stabilization threshold and yτy_{\tau} the agreement with asymptotic apportionment threshold defined in Lemmas 5.17 and 5.19. Define

yτmax:={∞if ​τ∈V,yτif ​τ∉V.y_{\tau}^{\max}:=\begin{cases}\infty&\text{if }\tau\in V,\\ y_{\tau}&\text{if }\tau\notin V.\end{cases}

Then the probability of a quota violation caused by a guaranteed seat is

Pr⁡(quota violation)=∫−1/31/3∫yτFyτmaxf​(x​(y,τ),y)​32​y​𝑑y​𝑑τ.\Pr(\text{quota violation})=\int_{-1/3}^{1/3}\int_{y_{\tau}^{F}}^{y_{\tau}^{\max}}f\bigl(x(y,\tau),\,y\bigr)\,\frac{3}{2}y\,dy\,d\tau.

3 Assumptions and Background Information

We assume M≥nM\geq n and no ties in priority values. The following are simple lemmas and known results:

Lemma 3.1.

[2] Divisor methods satisfy neutrality and proportionality: they are invariant under permutation of the states and under positive scaling of all populations.

Therefore, we may analyze just the standard quotas (q1,…,qn)(q_{1},\dots,q_{n}) or the reduced population vectors (1,p2p1,…,pnp1)\left(1,\frac{p_{2}}{p_{1}},\dots,\frac{p_{n}}{p_{1}}\right).

Theorem 3.2.

[2] No single divisor-method apportionment can simultaneously contain both an upper and a lower quota violation.

Theorem 3.3.

[2] When n=2n=2, no quota violations occur in any divisor method.

This paper builds off of the authors’ prior work. A main result is listed here for convenience which will be used later in the paper.

Theorem 3.4.

[13] (Lower Quota Violation Criteria Test) Let AA be Adams’s, Dean’s, or the Huntington-Hill method, with divisor function δ​(s)\delta(s). Order the populations such that p1<p2<p3p_{1}<p_{2}<p_{3} and calculate the standard quotas qiq_{i}. Then the apportionment A​(p1,p2,p3)A(p_{1},p_{2},p_{3}) on MM seats has a lower quota violation if and only if all three of the following statements are true:

  1. 1.

    q3​δ​(⌊q1⌋)<q1​δ​(⌊q3⌋−1)q_{3}\delta(\lfloor q_{1}\rfloor)<q_{1}\delta(\lfloor q_{3}\rfloor-1)

  2. 2.

    q3​δ​(⌊q2⌋)<q2​δ​(⌊q3⌋−1)q_{3}\delta(\lfloor q_{2}\rfloor)<q_{2}\delta(\lfloor q_{3}\rfloor-1)

  3. 3.

    ⌈q1⌉+⌈q2⌉+⌊q3⌋=M+1\lceil q_{1}\rceil+\lceil q_{2}\rceil+\lfloor q_{3}\rfloor=M+1 (equivalently for q1,q2∉ℤq_{1},q_{2}\notin\mathbb{Z}, ⌊q1⌋+⌊q2⌋+⌊q3⌋=M−1)\lfloor q_{1}\rfloor+\lfloor q_{2}\rfloor+\lfloor q_{3}\rfloor=M-1).

Remark 3.5.

By repeating the arguments used in [13], the above test still holds for modified Webster’s and modified Jefferson’s methods.

An immediate extension of Theorem 5.5 in [13] is:

Theorem 3.6.

Let AA be a divisor method with divisor function δ\delta satisfying δ​(0)=0\delta(0)=0 and x+1δ​(⌊x⌋)\dfrac{x+1}{\delta(\lfloor x\rfloor)} is a decreasing function for x∈[1,∞)x\in[1,\infty). Then, if A​(q1,q2,q3)A(q_{1},q_{2},q_{3}) produces a quota violation with q1<q2<q3q_{1}<q_{2}<q_{3}, A​(q1,q2,q3)=(⌈q1⌉,⌈q2⌉,⌊q3⌋−1)A(q_{1},q_{2},q_{3})=(\lceil q_{1}\rceil,\lceil q_{2}\rceil,\lfloor q_{3}\rfloor-1). In particular, this holds for Huntington-Hill’s, Adams’, Dean’s, modified Jefferson’s, and modified Webster’s methods.

4 Asymptotic Quota Stabilization via the τ\tau-Statistic

This section introduces a new statistic, τ\tau, which is useful for studying quota violations and quota violations caused by nonzero allocations. For simplicity, the section begins by discussing three states, and later explains how these results extend to nn states.

4.1 Defining Relative Skewness for n=3n=3 States

Definition 4.1.

Let (1,x,y)(1,x,y), 1<x<y1<x<y, be the associated reduced population vector for a three state distribution (p1,p2,p3p_{1},p_{2},p_{3}). Define the τ\tau statistic as follows:

τ:=mean​(1,x,y)−median​(1,x,y)max⁡(1,x,y).\tau:=\frac{\mathrm{mean}(1,x,y)-\mathrm{median}(1,x,y)}{\max(1,x,y)}.
Lemma 4.2.

The statistic τ\tau is preserved under scaling: τ​(1,x,y)=τ​(λ,λ​x,λ​y)\tau(1,x,y)=\tau(\lambda,\lambda x,\lambda y) for λ∈ℝ>0\lambda\in\mathbb{R}_{>0}. As such, τ\tau can be calculated for any three state distribution, not just reduced population vectors.

Proof.
τ​(λ,λ​x,λ​y)=λ+λ​x+λ​y3−λ​xλ​y=1+x+y3−xy=τ​(1,x,y).\tau(\lambda,\lambda x,\lambda y)=\frac{\frac{\lambda+\lambda x+\lambda y}{3}-\lambda x}{\lambda y}=\frac{\frac{1+x+y}{3}-x}{y}=\tau(1,x,y).

∎

Lemma 4.3.

The statistic τ\tau parametrizes a family of population distributions dependent on xx. Specifically, this is the set of reduced population vectors associated with a fixed τ\tau: {(1,x,y​(x,τ))∣x∈ℝ,x>1}\{(1,x,y(x,\tau))\mid x\in\mathbb{R},x>1\}, where yy is a linear function of xx.

Proof.

Fix τ\tau. Then, for some (1,x,y)(1,x,y) with 1<x<y1<x<y, by definition

τ=1+x+y3−xy\tau=\frac{\frac{1+x+y}{3}-x}{y}

Solving for yy gives

y​(x,τ)=2​x−11−3​τ=21−3​τ​x−11−3​τ.y(x,\tau)=\frac{2x-1}{1-3\tau}=\frac{2}{1-3\tau}x-\frac{1}{1-3\tau}.

Thus, for a fixed τ\tau, yy is a linear function of xx and the set of reduced population vectors forms a one-dimensional family in {(x,y)| 1<x<y}\{(x,y)\,|\,1<x<y\}. ∎

The last lemma allows us to not think of reduced population vectors (1,x,y)(1,x,y) as independent triplets but instead as parametrized rays, with a linear relationship between xx and yy, in the space of allowable reduced population vectors on the x​yxy-plane.

4.2 Properties and Bounds

Lemma 4.4.

For any three state distribution with reduced population (1,x,y)(1,x,y), with 1<x<y1<x<y, the statistic τ\tau satisfies the strict inequality |τ|<13|\tau|<\frac{1}{3}.

Proof.

Since x>1,1−2​x<−1x>1,1-2x<-1, and in particular, 1−2​x1-2x is strictly negative. Then from the definition of τ,\tau,

τ=1+x+y3−xy=1+x+y−3​x3​y=1−2​x+y3​y=13+1−2​x3​y<13.\tau=\dfrac{\dfrac{1+x+y}{3}-x}{y}=\dfrac{1+x+y-3x}{3y}=\dfrac{1-2x+y}{3y}=\dfrac{1}{3}+\dfrac{1-2x}{3y}<\dfrac{1}{3}.

To show the lower bound for τ\tau, start with the simplified expression τ=1−2​x+y3​y\tau=\dfrac{1-2x+y}{3y} and notice that τ\tau decreases as xx increases. Since x<yx<y, as x→y−,τ→1−2​y+y3​y=1−y3​y=13​y−13.x\rightarrow y^{-},\tau\rightarrow\dfrac{1-2y+y}{3y}=\dfrac{1-y}{3y}=\dfrac{1}{3y}-\dfrac{1}{3}. As y→∞y\rightarrow\infty, τ→−13\tau\rightarrow-\dfrac{1}{3}. Thus τ>−13\tau>-\frac{1}{3} for any 1<x<y1<x<y.

∎

Remark 4.5.

The value of τ\tau effectively measures the relative position of the median xx between the minimum and the maximum values. When τ\tau is close to its upper bound of 13\frac{1}{3}, xx is as small as possible (close to 1.) This represents a right-skewed population distribution. Similarly, when τ\tau is close to its lower bound of −13-\frac{1}{3}, xx is as large as possible (close to yy), and represents a left-skewed population vector. When τ=0\tau=0, the mean and median are equal, and from the formula for yy in terms of xx and τ\tau, y=2​x−1y=2x-1. Equivalently, x=1+y2x=\frac{1+y}{2}, the average of 1 and yy.

The initial space of population vectors is three-dimensional: (p1,p2,p3).(p_{1},p_{2},p_{3}). Scaling reduces this space to two dimensions: (1,x,y)(1,x,y). Introducing τ\tau, a measure of skewness, replaces yy, a measure of the relative size of the largest state to the smallest state.

4.3 Asymptotic Stability and the Apportionment Limit

Since the reduced population vector (1,x,y​(x,τ))(1,x,y(x,\tau)) depends continuously on xx (and on τ\tau), the next theorem will show the limiting behavior as x→∞x\to\infty is determined entirely by taking the limit of the vector of standard quotas, except possibly at the finitely many τ\tau-values where priority values become equal.

Theorem 4.6.

(Asymptotic Apportionment) Let (1,x,y​(x,τ))(1,x,y(x,\tau)) be a reduced population vector associated with a fixed τ\tau, and let AA be a divisor method that guarantees nonzero allocations on MM seats. Then, for all but finitely many τ∈(−13,13)\tau\in(-\frac{1}{3},\frac{1}{3}):

limx→∞A​(1,x,y​(x,τ))=A~​(M​(1−3​τ)3−3​τ,2​M3−3​τ),\displaystyle{\lim_{x\to\infty}A(1,x,y(x,\tau))=\tilde{A}\left(\frac{M(1-3\tau)}{3-3\tau},\frac{2M}{3-3\tau}\right)},

where A~\tilde{A} is defined as apportioning the remaining M−1M-1 seats among states 2 and 3 according to the divisor method AA (see definition 1.1).

Proof.

For a fixed τ\tau and reduced population vector (1,x,y​(x,τ))=(1,x,2​x−11−3​τ)(1,x,y(x,\tau))=\left(1,x,\frac{2x-1}{1-3\tau}\right), the total population is P=1+x+2​x−11−3​τ=(3−3​τ)​x−3​τ1−3​τ.P=1+x+\frac{2x-1}{1-3\tau}=\frac{(3-3\tau)x-3\tau}{1-3\tau}. Calculate the standard quotas qi=M​piPq_{i}=\frac{Mp_{i}}{P}:

(q1,q2,q3)=(M​(1−3​τ)(3−3​τ)​x−3​τ,M​(1−3​τ)​x(3−3​τ)​x−3​τ,M​(2​x−1)(3−3​τ)​x−3​τ)(q_{1},q_{2},q_{3})=\left(\frac{M(1-3\tau)}{(3-3\tau)x-3\tau},\frac{M(1-3\tau)x}{(3-3\tau)x-3\tau},\frac{M(2x-1)}{(3-3\tau)x-3\tau}\right)

Then, as x→∞x\to\infty

(q1,q2,q3)→(0,M​(1−3​τ)3−3​τ,2​M3−3​τ).(q_{1},q_{2},q_{3})\to\left(0,\frac{M(1-3\tau)}{3-3\tau},\frac{2M}{3-3\tau}\right).

Divisor methods are piecewise constant functions on open regions of the population space. The boundaries of these regions are hypersurfaces defined by equalities of the priority values, piδ​(k)=pjδ​(l)\frac{p_{i}}{\delta(k)}=\frac{p_{j}}{\delta(l)}, with 0≤k,l≤M−10\leq k,l\leq M-1. The boundaries cause a discontinuity in AA and a change in apportionment. The reduced population vector (1,x,y)(1,x,y) depends continuously on τ\tau (and on xx). There are finitely many such equations, each rational functions in τ\tau. After clearing denominators, each condition yields a linear equation in τ\tau and has at most one solution. Therefore, the exceptional set of τ\tau is finite. Define this set as ℰ\mathcal{E}.

For τ∉ℰ\tau\not\in\mathcal{E}, AA is constant on a neighborhood near (0,M​(1−3​τ)3−3​τ,2​M3−3​τ)\left(0,\frac{M(1-3\tau)}{3-3\tau},\frac{2M}{3-3\tau}\right), and therefore

limx→∞A​(1,x,y​(x,τ))=limx→∞A​(q1,q2,q3)=A​(limx→∞(q1,q2,q3))=A​(0,M​(1−3​τ)3−3​τ,2​M3−3​τ).\begin{array}[]{cl}\displaystyle\lim_{x\to\infty}A(1,x,y(x,\tau))&=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to\infty}A(q_{1},q_{2},q_{3})\\[9.0pt] &=A(\displaystyle\lim_{x\to\infty}(q_{1},q_{2},q_{3}))\\[9.0pt] &=A\left(0,\frac{M(1-3\tau)}{3-3\tau},\frac{2M}{3-3\tau}\right).\end{array}

As AA guarantees strictly positive apportionments, state one receives at least one seat. Because q1​(x)→0q_{1}(x)\to 0, state 1’s priorities become negligible so state one only receives one seat and the remaining M−1M-1 seats are apportioned exactly as if AA were applied only to states 22 and 33. This is precisely the definition of A~​(M​(1−3​τ)3−3​τ,2​M3−3​τ),\tilde{A}\left(\frac{M(1-3\tau)}{3-3\tau},\frac{2M}{3-3\tau}\right), completing the proof. ∎

In what follows, we introduce new notation for the limiting standard quotas:

q~2:=M​(1−3​τ)3−3​τ,q~3:=2​M3−3​τ.\tilde{q}_{2}:=\frac{M(1-3\tau)}{3-3\tau},\qquad\tilde{q}_{3}:=\frac{2M}{3-3\tau}.

For τ∉ℰ\tau\not\in\mathcal{E}, both q~2\tilde{q}_{2} and q~3\tilde{q}_{3} are nonintegral limit points of the standard quotas q2​(x)q_{2}(x) and q3​(x)q_{3}(x). As such, their integer parts must stabilize. Consequently, the asymptotic quota behavior of the full three-state apportionment is determined entirely by which of states 2 or 3 receives the final seat from A~.\tilde{A}. The following theorem formalizes this stabilization and shows that, beyond a finite threshold, quota compliance or violation is completely determined by τ\tau.

Theorem 4.7.

(Asymptotic Quota Stabilization for Nonexceptional τ\tau) Let ℰ\mathcal{E} be the exceptional set of finite τ∈(−13,13)\tau\in(-\frac{1}{3},\frac{1}{3}) such that Theorem 4.6 fails. Fix τ∉ℰ\tau\notin\mathcal{E}. Then there exists some xτx_{\tau} such that for all x≥xτx\geq x_{\tau}, A​(1,x,y​(x,τ))A(1,x,y(x,\tau)) is always a lower quota violation caused by nonzero allocation or always not a violation, with this behavior determined by τ\tau.

Specifically, let state i∈{2,3}i\in\{2,3\} be the state that receives the last seat when apportioning MM seats among only states 22 and 33 from A​(q~2,q~3)A(\tilde{q}_{2},\tilde{q}_{3}). Then:

  1. 1.

    If A​(q~2,q~3)A(\tilde{q}_{2},\tilde{q}_{3}) assigns state ii its lower quota ⌊q~i⌋\lfloor\tilde{q}_{i}\rfloor, then for all x≥xτx\geq x_{\tau}, the apportionment A​(1,x,y​(x,τ))A(1,x,y(x,\tau)) is a lower quota violation caused by nonzero allocations, with state ii receiving ⌊q~i⌋−1\lfloor\tilde{q}_{i}\rfloor-1 seats.

  2. 2.

    If A​(q~2,q~3)A(\tilde{q}_{2},\tilde{q}_{3}) assigns state ii its upper quota ⌈q~i⌉\lceil\tilde{q}_{i}\rceil, then for all x≥xτx\geq x_{\tau}, the apportionment A​(1,x,y​(x,τ))A(1,x,y(x,\tau)) is not a quota violation.

Proof.

Theorem 4.6 implies

limx→∞A​(1,x,y​(x,τ))=A~​(M​(1−3​τ)3−3​τ,2​M3−3​τ):=A~​(q~2,q~3)\lim_{x\to\infty}A(1,x,y(x,\tau))=\tilde{A}\left(\frac{M(1-3\tau)}{3-3\tau},\frac{2M}{3-3\tau}\right):=\tilde{A}(\tilde{q}_{2},\tilde{q}_{3})

By the definition of a limit, there must exist some point in which A​(1,x,y​(x,τ))A(1,x,y(x,\tau)) is epsilon close to A~​(q~2,q~3)\tilde{A}(\tilde{q}_{2},\tilde{q}_{3}). Since AA and A~\tilde{A} only output integer vectors in ℤ3\mathbb{Z}^{3}, convergence implies there exists some xτAx_{\tau}^{A} such that

A​(1,x,y​(x,τ))=A~​(q~2,q~3)for all ​x≥xτA.A(1,x,y(x,\tau))=\tilde{A}(\tilde{q}_{2},\tilde{q}_{3})\qquad\text{for all }x\geq x^{A}_{\tau}.

Since τ∉ℰ\tau\notin\mathcal{E}, the limiting quotas

q~2=M​(1−3​τ)3−3​τ,q~3=2​M3−3​τ\tilde{q}_{2}=\frac{M(1-3\tau)}{3-3\tau},\qquad\tilde{q}_{3}=\frac{2M}{3-3\tau}

are not integers so each lies at positive distance from the nearest integer. Because the corresponding standard quotas (q2​(x),q3​(x))(q_{2}(x),q_{3}(x)) converge to (q~2,q~3)(\tilde{q}_{2},\tilde{q}_{3}) as x→∞x\to\infty, there exists δ>0\delta>0 and xτFx_{\tau}^{F} such that for all x≥xτFx\geq x_{\tau}^{F} the varying quotas remain within δ\delta of their limits and therefore lie in the same unit intervals as q~2\tilde{q}_{2} and q~3\tilde{q}_{3}. It follows that for all x≥xτFx\geq x_{\tau}^{F},

⌊q2​(x)⌋=⌊q~2⌋and⌊q3​(x)⌋=⌊q~3⌋,\lfloor q_{2}(x)\rfloor=\lfloor\tilde{q}_{2}\rfloor\qquad\text{and}\qquad\lfloor q_{3}(x)\rfloor=\lfloor\tilde{q}_{3}\rfloor,

so the integer parts of the quotas stabilize.

Define xτ=max⁡{xτA,xτF}x_{\tau}=\max\{x_{\tau}^{A},x_{\tau}^{F}\}. Then for all x≥xτx\geq x_{\tau},

A​(1,x,y​(x,τ))=A~​(q~2,q~3)​ and ​⌊qi​(x)⌋=⌊q~i⌋,i=2,3.A(1,x,y(x,\tau))=\tilde{A}(\tilde{q}_{2},\tilde{q}_{3})\text{ and }\lfloor q_{i}(x)\rfloor=\lfloor\tilde{q}_{i}\rfloor,i=2,3.

Therefore whether A​(1,x,y​(x,τ))A(1,x,y(x,\tau)) violates lower quota is equivalent to whether the limiting apportionment does.

Finally, A​(q~2,q~3)=(a2,a3)A(\tilde{q}_{2},\tilde{q}_{3})=(a_{2},a_{3}) apportions M−1M-1 seats, and A~\tilde{A} assigns the remaining seat to state 1. Let state ii receive the final seat in the apportionment of MM seats in A​(q~2,q~3)A(\tilde{q}_{2},\tilde{q}_{3}). Then, since for two states there are no quota violations by Theorem 3.3 there are only two possible cases:

  1. 1.

    If ai=⌊q~i⌋a_{i}=\lfloor\tilde{q}_{i}\rfloor, the final apportionment for state ii is ⌊q~i⌋−1\lfloor\tilde{q}_{i}\rfloor-1, which is a lower quota violation caused by nonzero allocations.

  2. 2.

    If ai=⌈qi~⌉a_{i}=\lceil\tilde{q_{i}}\rceil, the final apportionment for state ii is ⌊q~i⌋\lfloor\tilde{q}_{i}\rfloor, which is not a quota violation.

Because the limiting quotas (q~2,q~3)(\tilde{q}_{2},\tilde{q}_{3}) are functions of τ\tau, and the two-state allocation A​(q~2,q~3)A(\tilde{q}_{2},\tilde{q}_{3}) depends only on these values, the presence or absence of a lower quota violation is uniquely determined by τ\tau as claimed. ∎

This theorem then motivates the following definitions:

Definition 4.8.

A value τ∉ℰ\tau\notin\mathcal{E} is said to be ultimately-violatory if for x≥xτx\geq x_{\tau}, A​(1,x,y​(τ,x))A(1,x,y(\tau,x)) produces a quota violation. Otherwise τ\tau is said to be ultimately non-violatory or ultimately quota compliant.

Additionally, note the structure of xτx_{\tau}:

Remark 4.9.

The τ\tau-interval (−13,13)(-\frac{1}{3},\frac{1}{3}) can be decomposed into finitely many intervals such that on each interval, xτx_{\tau} is locally represented in the form xτ=ab+c​τ+d​τb+c​τ+ex_{\tau}=\frac{a}{b+c\tau}+\frac{d\tau}{b+c\tau}+e for a,b,c,d,e∈ℝa,b,c,d,e\in\mathbb{R} and denominators not identically 0. This is proven later in Theorem 5.20.

4.4 Extension to nn States

For three states, the statistic τ\tau parametrizes one-dimensional families of reduced population vectors (1,x,y)(1,x,y) along which the relative proportions of the populations vary linearly. These families appear as straight rays in the (x,y)(x,y)-plane and are useful because apportionment behavior stabilizes along such rays as x→∞x\to\infty.

This idea may be generalized to nn states, resulting in a similar theory which is presented briefly here. Note that proofs in this section are omitted due to similarity with the three state case.

Definition 4.10.

Given a reduced population vector (1,x,y1​…,yn−2)(1,x,y_{1}\dots,y_{n-2}), define 𝝉\bm{\tau} vector as:

𝝉=(τ1,…,τn−2)=(1+x+y13−xy1,…,1+x+yn−23−xyn−2).\bm{\tau}=(\tau_{1},\dots,\tau_{n-2})=\left(\frac{\frac{1+x+y_{1}}{3}-x}{y_{1}},\dots,\frac{\frac{1+x+y_{n-2}}{3}-x}{y_{n-2}}\right).
Lemma 4.11.

The vector 𝛕=(τ1,…,τn−2)\bm{\tau}=(\tau_{1},\dots,\tau_{n-2}) satisfies −13<τ1<⋯<τn−2<13-\frac{1}{3}<\tau_{1}<\dots<\tau_{n-2}<\frac{1}{3} under the ordering (1<x<y1<…<yn−2)(1<x<y_{1}<\ldots<y_{n-2}).

Lemma 4.12.

Given 𝛕=(τ1,…,τn−2)\bm{\tau}=(\tau_{1},\dots,\tau_{n-2}), 𝛕\bm{\tau} parameterize a family of population vectors

(1,x,y1​(x,τ1),…,yn−2​(x,τn−2)),where yi​(x,τi)=2​x−11−3​τi.(1,x,y_{1}(x,\tau_{1}),\dots,y_{n-2}(x,\tau_{n-2})),\qquad\text{where }\qquad y_{i}(x,\tau_{i})=\frac{2x-1}{1-3\tau_{i}}.
Theorem 4.13.

(Asymptotic Apportionment) Let (1,x,y1​(x,τ1),…,yn−2​(x,τn−2))(1,x,y_{1}(x,\tau_{1}),\ldots,y_{n-2}(x,\tau_{n-2})) be a reduced population vector with 𝛕=(τ1,…,τn−2)\bm{\tau}=(\tau_{1},\ldots,\tau_{n-2}). Let AA be a divisor method that guarantees nonzero allocations on MM seats. Then for almost every 𝛕\bm{\tau} satisfying −13<τ1<⋯<τn−2<13,-\tfrac{1}{3}<\tau_{1}<\cdots<\tau_{n-2}<\tfrac{1}{3},

limx→∞A​(1,x,y1​(x,τ1),…,yn−2​(x,τn−2))=A~1​(q~2,…,q~n).\lim_{x\to\infty}A(1,x,y_{1}(x,\tau_{1}),\dots,y_{n-2}(x,\tau_{n-2}))=\tilde{A}_{1}(\tilde{q}_{2},\dots,\tilde{q}_{n}).

The limiting quotas are

q~2=limx→∞M​xP​(x),q~i+2=limx→∞M​yi​(x,τi)P​(x),\tilde{q}_{2}=\lim_{x\to\infty}\frac{Mx}{P(x)},\qquad\tilde{q}_{i+2}=\lim_{x\to\infty}\frac{My_{i}(x,\tau_{i})}{P(x)},

where P​(x)=1+x+y1​(x,τ1)+⋯+yn−2​(x,τn−2).P(x)=1+x+y_{1}(x,\tau_{1})+\cdots+y_{n-2}(x,\tau_{n-2}).

Theorem 4.14.

(Asymptotic Quota Stabilization) Let 𝛕\bm{\tau} be a nonexceptional parameter vector for which Theorem 4.13 holds. Then there exists x𝛕x_{\bm{\tau}} such that for all x≥x𝛕x\geq x_{\bm{\tau}}

A​(1,x,y1​(x,τ1),…,yn−2​(x,τn−2))=A~1​(q~2,…,q~n).A(1,x,y_{1}(x,\tau_{1}),\dots,y_{n-2}(x,\tau_{n-2}))=\tilde{A}_{1}(\tilde{q}_{2},\dots,\tilde{q}_{n}).

Consequently the quota behavior stabilizes: for sufficiently large xx, the apportionment is always either quota compliant, a quota violation caused by nonzero allocation, a quota violation worsened by nonzero allocation, or an upper quota violation. Moreover, this behavior depends only on 𝛕\bm{\tau}. If A​(q~2,…,q~n)=(a~2,…,a~n)A(\tilde{q}_{2},\dots,\tilde{q}_{n})=(\tilde{a}_{2},\dots,\tilde{a}_{n}) is the apportionment of MM seats among states 2,…,n2,\dots,n and state ii receive the last seat then:

  1. 1.

    If A​(q~2,…,q~n)A(\tilde{q}_{2},\dots,\tilde{q}_{n}) is not a quota violation and a~i=⌊q~i⌋\tilde{a}_{i}=\lfloor\tilde{q}_{i}\rfloor, then the asymptotic apportionment is a quota violation caused by nonzero allocation.

  2. 2.

    If A​(q~2,…,q~n)A(\tilde{q}_{2},\dots,\tilde{q}_{n}) is not a quota violation and a~i=⌈q~i⌉\tilde{a}_{i}=\lceil\tilde{q}_{i}\rceil, then the asymptotic apportionment is not a quota violation.

  3. 3.

    If A​(q~2,…,q~n)A(\tilde{q}_{2},\dots,\tilde{q}_{n}) is an upper quota violation where state ii is the only state assigned more than its upper quota and a~i=⌈q~i⌉+1\tilde{a}_{i}=\lceil\tilde{q}_{i}\rceil+1, then the asymptotic apportionment is not a quota violation.

  4. 4.

    If A​(q~2,…,q~n)A(\tilde{q}_{2},\dots,\tilde{q}_{n}) is an upper quota violation and case (3) does not apply, then the asymptotic apportionment is an upper quota violation.

  5. 5.

    If A​(q~2,…,q~n)A(\tilde{q}_{2},\dots,\tilde{q}_{n}) is a lower quota violation, then the asymptotic apportionment is a quota violation worsened by nonzero allocation.

5 Probability of Quota Violations Caused by Nonzero Allocation in Three States

In this section, τ\tau is used to derive a formula for probability of a quota violation caused by nonzero allocations for n=3n=3 states. We begin by studying the case in which reduced population vectors are drawn uniformly from {(1,x,y)∣1<x<y}\{(1,x,y)\mid 1<x<y\}, before expanding the analysis to more general distributions.

Throughout, AA denotes either the Huntington-Hill, Adams, Dean, modified Webster or modified Jefferson’s method. The argument presented here can be extended to additional divisor methods with minor modifications.

5.1 The Asymptotic Uniform Distribution

The following result characterizes τ\tau when a reduced population vector (1,x,y)(1,x,y) is drawn from the region {(1,x,y)∣1<x<y}\{(1,x,y)\mid 1<x<y\} under an asymptotic uniform distribution. Heuristically the drawing is from {(1,x,y)∣1<x<y<h}\{(1,x,y)\mid 1<x<y<h\} where hh is large. Formally, the definition of the probability for a set EE is

P​(E)=limh→∞Ph​(E)P(E)=\lim_{h\to\infty}P_{h}(E)

where Ph​(E)P_{h}(E) is the probability of EE under a uniform distribution on {(1,x,y)∣1<x<y<h}\{(1,x,y)\mid 1<x<y<h\}.

Lemma 5.1.

Under the asymptotic uniform distribution defined above on reduced population vectors {(1,x,y)∣1<x<y}\{(1,x,y)\mid 1<x<y\}, the parameter τ\tau is a uniform random variable on (−13,13)\left(-\frac{1}{3},\frac{1}{3}\right).

Proof.

It suffices to show that for every k∈ℝk\in\mathbb{R}, the tail probability P​(τ>k)P(\tau>k) coincides with that of a Uniform(−13,13)\left(-\frac{1}{3},\frac{1}{3}\right) random variable. The only unknown probability is when τ∈(−13,13)\tau\in(-\frac{1}{3},\frac{1}{3}) by Lemma 4.4:

P​(τ>k)={1k≤−13?k∈(−13,13)0k≥13P(\tau>k)=\left\{\begin{array}[]{lcr}1&k\leq-\frac{1}{3}\\ ?&k\in\left(-\frac{1}{3},\frac{1}{3}\right)\\ 0&k\geq\frac{1}{3}\end{array}\right.

To find the unknown expression, let k∈(−13,13)k\in\left(-\frac{1}{3},\frac{1}{3}\right). Then

P​(τ>k)=P​(1+x+y3−xy>k)=P​(x<(12−32​k)​y+12).P\left(\tau>k\right)=P\left(\frac{\frac{1+x+y}{3}-x}{y}>k\right)=P\left(x<\left(\frac{1}{2}-\frac{3}{2}k\right)y+\frac{1}{2}\right).

This can then be interpreted geometrically as the fraction of the region 1<x<y1<x<y covered by x<(12−32​k)​y+12x<\left(\frac{1}{2}-\frac{3}{2}k\right)y+\frac{1}{2}. For small yy, the region x<(12−32​k)​y+12x<\left(\frac{1}{2}-\frac{3}{2}k\right)y+\frac{1}{2} may lie below x=1x=1, so the lower bound in yy is found by solving (12−32​k)​y+12=1\left(\frac{1}{2}-\frac{3}{2}k\right)y+\frac{1}{2}=1, which gives y=11−3​k.y=\frac{1}{1-3k}. For k∈(−13,13)k\in\left(-\frac{1}{3},\frac{1}{3}\right), the line x=(12−32​k)​y+12x=\left(\frac{1}{2}-\frac{3}{2}k\right)y+\frac{1}{2} has slope strictly less than 11, so for sufficiently large yy it lies below the line x=yx=y. For sufficiently large yy, the line determines the upper boundary in xx and the contribution from the region where x=yx=y bounds the domain is asymptotically negligible. Accordingly, the yy–integration extends to hh.

Thus, the desired probability equals

P​(τ>k)=P​(x<(12−32​k)​y+12)=limh→∞∫11−3​kh(12−32​k)​y−12​d​y∫1h(y−1)​𝑑y=12−32​k\displaystyle P(\tau>k)=P\left(x<\left(\frac{1}{2}-\frac{3}{2}k\right)y+\frac{1}{2}\right)=\lim_{h\to\infty}\frac{\int_{\frac{1}{1-3k}}^{h}\left(\frac{1}{2}-\frac{3}{2}k\right)y-\frac{1}{2}\,dy}{\int_{1}^{h}(y-1)\,dy}=\frac{1}{2}-\frac{3}{2}k

The last equality follows since both the numerator and the denominator are quadratic polynomials in hh, so the limit is determined by the leading coefficients. For k∈(−13,13)k\in\left(-\frac{1}{3},\frac{1}{3}\right), this coincides with the tail probability of a Uniform(−13,13)\left(-\frac{1}{3},\frac{1}{3}\right) random variable. ∎

The uniform distribution of τ\tau obtained above is not merely an result of the specific area normalization on the wedge {1<x<y}\{1<x<y\}. In fact, the above calculation reveals that the distributional behavior of τ\tau depends primarily on the asymptotic distribution of the ratio x/yx/y. The following proposition makes this precise.

Proposition 5.2 (Robustness of the Uniform Distribution of τ\tau).

Suppose reduced population vectors are sampled for which u=xyu=\frac{x}{y} is asymptotically uniformly distributed on (0,1)(0,1) and y→∞y\to\infty in density. Then the induced distribution τ=1−2​x+y3​y\tau=\frac{1-2x+y}{3y} converges to the uniform distribution on (−13,13)\left(-\frac{1}{3},\frac{1}{3}\right).

Proof.

Writing x=u​yx=uy, we have τ=1−2​u​y+y3​y=13​y+1−2​u3.\tau=\frac{1-2uy+y}{3y}=\frac{1}{3y}+\frac{1-2u}{3}. As y→∞y\to\infty, the term 13​y\frac{1}{3y} vanishes in probability. Thus the limiting distribution of τ\tau coincides with that of 1−2​u3.\frac{1-2u}{3}. Since uu is asymptotically uniform on (0,1)(0,1), it follows that 1−2​u3∼Unif​(−13,13)\frac{1-2u}{3}\sim\mathrm{Unif}\!\left(-\frac{1}{3},\frac{1}{3}\right). ∎

Having established that τ\tau is uniformly distributed on (−13,13)\left(-\tfrac{1}{3},\tfrac{1}{3}\right), we now relate the behavior of quota violations to the distribution of τ\tau. The following lemma shows that the problem reduces to identifying which values of τ\tau are ultimately violatory.

Theorem 5.3 (Quota Violations Under the Asymptotic Uniform Distribution).

Under the asymptotic uniform distribution on reduced population vectors (1,x,y)(1,x,y) with 1<x<y1<x<y the following are equivalent:

  • •

    P​(A​(1,x,y)​ is a quota violation).P(A(1,x,y)\text{ is a quota violation}).

  • •

    P(A(1,x,y) is a quota violation caused by nonzero allocations).P(A(1,x,y)\text{ is a quota violation caused by nonzero allocations)}.

  • •

    P​(τ​(x,y)​ is ultimately violatory).P(\tau(x,y)\text{ is ultimately violatory}).

Proof.

It follows from Theorem 4.7 and the definition of ultimately violatory that the following are equivalent:

  • •

    P​(A​(1,x,y)​ is a quota violation for x>xτ)P(A(1,x,y)\text{ is a quota violation for $x>x_{\tau}$})

  • •

    P(A(1,x,y) is a quota violation caused by nonzero allocations for x>xτ)P(A(1,x,y)\text{ is a quota violation caused by nonzero allocations for $x>x_{\tau}$)}

  • •

    P​(τ​(x,y)​ is ultimately violatory for x>xτ).P(\tau(x,y)\text{ is ultimately violatory for $x>x_{\tau}$}).

As such, it suffices to show P​(x<xτ)=0P(x<x_{\tau})=0, as then the desired statement may be deduced by adding 0.

To start, let Ph​(E)P_{h}(E) be the probability of EE under the uniform distribution on {(1,x,y)∣1<x<y<h}\{(1,x,y)\mid 1<x<y<h\} and let r∈(1,h)r\in(1,h). Then:

0≤Ph​(x<xτ)≤Ph​(τ​∣xτ>​r)+Ph​(x<r).0\leq P_{h}(x<x_{\tau})\leq P_{h}(\tau\mid x_{\tau}>r)+P_{h}(x<r).

It follows from Remark 4.9 that for each ε>0\varepsilon>0, there exists rεr_{\varepsilon} such that the total length of the set of τ\tau such that xτ>rεx_{\tau}>r_{\varepsilon} is less than (23)⋅(ε2)\left(\frac{2}{3}\right)\cdot\left(\frac{\varepsilon}{2}\right). Then, by Lemma 5.1, Ph​(xτ>rε)P_{h}(x_{\tau}>r_{\varepsilon}) converges to 32\frac{3}{2} times the total length of such τ\tau and therefore for all hh sufficiently large:

Ph​(τ​∣xτ>​rε)<ε2.P_{h}(\tau\mid x_{\tau}>r_{\varepsilon})<\frac{\varepsilon}{2}.

Additionally compute using the area of triangles and squares:

Ph​(x<rε)=2​(h−1)​(rε−1)−(rε−1)2(h−1)2P_{h}(x<r_{\varepsilon})=\frac{2(h-1)(r_{\varepsilon}-1)-(r_{\varepsilon}-1)^{2}}{(h-1)^{2}}

which goes to 0 as h→∞h\to\infty. Thus, for all hh sufficiently large:

Ph​(x<rε)<ε2.P_{h}(x<r_{\varepsilon})<\frac{\varepsilon}{2}.

Then for all hh sufficiently large:

0≤Ph​(x<xτ)≤Ph​(τ​∣xτ>​rε)+Ph​(x<rε)<ε.0\leq P_{h}(x<x_{\tau})\leq P_{h}(\tau\mid x_{\tau}>r_{\varepsilon})+P_{h}(x<r_{\varepsilon})<\varepsilon.

So

P​(x<xτ)=limh→∞Ph​(x<xτ)=0,P(x<x_{\tau})=\lim_{h\to\infty}P_{h}(x<x_{\tau})=0,

as needed.

∎

5.2 An Asymptotic Probability Function for Three States

Fix MM seats and n=3n=3 states. By Theorem 5.3, to derive a probability function for quota violations under uniformly distributed population vectors, it suffices to determine precisely which values of τ\tau cause families to be violatory for all x≥xτx\geq x_{\tau}. Once the subset of admissible τ\tau is determined, the desired probability is then the length of this subset divided by the length of all possible τ\tau, which is 23\frac{2}{3} by Lemma 4.4.

As shown in Section 4, the three-state problem reduces to a two-state problem. In particular, there is a bijection between τ\tau and the standard quota q~3.\tilde{q}_{3}. For each τ\tau, the reduced population vector (1,x,y​(x,τ))(1,x,y(x,\tau)) has the same eventual apportionment behavior (for x≥xτx\geq x_{\tau}) to that of (q~2,q~3)(\tilde{q}_{2},\tilde{q}_{3}) by Theorem 4.7.

The relationship between τ\tau and q~3\tilde{q}_{3} are given by Theorem 4.6:

q~3=2​M3−3​τ,q~2+q~3=M\tilde{q}_{3}=\frac{2M}{3-3\tau},\qquad\tilde{q}_{2}+\tilde{q}_{3}=M

Consequently,

τ=1−2​M3​q~3.\tau=1-\frac{2M}{3\tilde{q}_{3}}.

Thus specifying q~3\tilde{q}_{3} determines τ\tau and q~2\tilde{q}_{2}, and vice versa. By construction, q~3>q~2\tilde{q}_{3}>\tilde{q}_{2}.

By Theorem 4.7, a value of τ\tau produces a lower quota violation for the reduced population vector if and only if the corresponding two state apportionment A~​(q~2,q~3)=A~​(M−q~3,q~3)\tilde{A}(\tilde{q}_{2},\tilde{q}_{3})=\tilde{A}(M-\tilde{q}_{3},\tilde{q}_{3}) produces a lower quota violation. We now determine precisely for which values of q~3\tilde{q}_{3} a lower violation occurs.

Write q~3=⌊q~3⌋+d\tilde{q}_{3}=\lfloor\tilde{q}_{3}\rfloor+d for d∈[0,1)d\in[0,1). The occurrence of a lower quota violation is determined by the apportionment of the final seat as d→1−d\to 1^{-}. Specifically, by comparing the priority values that determine if the last seat is assigned to the state at ⌊q~2⌋\lfloor\tilde{q}_{2}\rfloor or ⌈q~2⌉\lceil\tilde{q}_{2}\rceil, then we can determine exactly when a lower quota violation occurs in AA. When AA assigns the final seat to the first state, if the seat is removed from either the floor of q~2\tilde{q}_{2} or q~3\tilde{q}_{3} then a lower quota violation will occur. If the last seat is removed for the first state from either the ceiling of q~2\tilde{q}_{2} or q~2\tilde{q}_{2}, then no lower quota violation will occur.

Lemma 5.4.

Using the notation from above, when q~3=⌊q~3⌋\tilde{q}_{3}=\lfloor\tilde{q}_{3}\rfloor (or equivalently when d=0d=0), there is lower quota violation in A~​(M−q~3,q~3)\tilde{A}(M-\tilde{q}_{3},\tilde{q}_{3}).

Proof.

Suppose d=0d=0. Then q~3=⌊q~3⌋\tilde{q}_{3}=\lfloor\tilde{q}_{3}\rfloor is an integer. Since q~2=M−q~3\tilde{q}_{2}=M-\tilde{q}_{3}, it follows that q~2\tilde{q}_{2} is also an integer. When both quotas are integers, the two state apportionment agrees exactly with the quota vector: A~​(q~2,q~3)=(q~2,q~3).\tilde{A}(\tilde{q}_{2},\tilde{q}_{3})=(\tilde{q}_{2},\tilde{q}_{3}).

Therefore, in the corresponding three–state apportionment, state 1 must receive one seat, and one seat must be removed from either state 2 or state 3. Because q~2\tilde{q}_{2} and q~3\tilde{q}_{3} are integers, removing one seat from either state forces that state to receive strictly fewer seats than its lower quota. Therefore a lower quota violation occurs in A~​(M−q~3,q~3)\tilde{A}(M-\tilde{q}_{3},\tilde{q}_{3}). ∎

Lemma 5.5.

Let q~3=⌊q~3⌋+d\tilde{q}_{3}=\lfloor\tilde{q}_{3}\rfloor+d, for d∈[0,1)d\in[0,1). There exist numbers

0≤d∗≤d∗∗≤10\leq d^{*}\leq d^{**}\leq 1

such that [0,1)[0,1) decomposes into three intervals

I1=[0,d∗),I2=[d∗,d∗∗),I3=[d∗∗,1),I_{1}=[0,d^{*}),\qquad I_{2}=[d^{*},d^{**}),\qquad I_{3}=[d^{**},1),

with the following properties:

  1. 1.

    For d∈I1d\in I_{1}, a lower quota violation occurs in A~​(M−q~3,q~3)\tilde{A}(M-\tilde{q}_{3},\tilde{q}_{3}).

  2. 2.

    For d∈I2∪I3d\in I_{2}\cup I_{3}, no lower quota violation occurs in A~​(M−q~3,q~3)\tilde{A}(M-\tilde{q}_{3},\tilde{q}_{3}).

Proof.

By Lemma 5.4, when d=0d=0, a lower quota violation occurs. As dd increases, a lower quota violation continues to occur while the third state’s priority values increase and the second state’s priority values decrease. For these values of dd close to zero, a2=⌈q~2⌉a_{2}=\lceil\tilde{q}_{2}\rceil and a3=⌊q~2⌋a_{3}=\lfloor\tilde{q}_{2}\rfloor, with a3a_{3} assigned last by AA. Eventually, for some dd, the priority value that puts state three at its lower quota becomes less than the priority value that puts state two at its upper quota. Define d∗d^{*} to be the first value of dd where this happens and define the interval I1=[0,d∗)I_{1}=[0,d^{*}). For d∈I1d\in I_{1}, a2=⌈q~2⌉a_{2}=\lceil\tilde{q}_{2}\rceil and a3=⌊q~3⌋a_{3}=\lfloor\tilde{q}_{3}\rfloor, with a3a_{3} assigned last by AA. Then A~\tilde{A} results in the final apportionment with a3=⌊q~3⌋−1a_{3}=\lfloor\tilde{q}_{3}\rfloor-1, a lower quota violation.

For d≥d∗d\geq d^{*}, AA assigns a2=⌈q~2⌉a_{2}=\lceil\tilde{q}_{2}\rceil and a3=⌊q~3⌋a_{3}=\lfloor\tilde{q}_{3}\rfloor, with a2a_{2} assigned last, until there is some dd that causes state three to be apportioned its upper quota and state two to be apportioned its lower quota, with the last seat assigned to state three by AA. Call the first value of dd where this happens d∗∗d^{**} and the interval I2=[d∗,d∗∗)I_{2}=[d^{*},d^{**}). For d∈I2d\in I_{2}, a2=⌈q~2⌉a_{2}=\lceil\tilde{q}_{2}\rceil and a3=⌊q~3⌋a_{3}=\lfloor\tilde{q}_{3}\rfloor, with a2a_{2} assigned last by AA. Then A~\tilde{A} results in the final apportionment with a2=⌊q~2⌋a_{2}=\lfloor\tilde{q}_{2}\rfloor, which is a not lower quota violation.

Define I3=[d∗∗,1).I_{3}=[d^{**},1). For d∈I3d\in I_{3}, a2=⌊q~2⌋a_{2}=\lfloor\tilde{q}_{2}\rfloor and a3=⌈q~3⌉a_{3}=\lceil\tilde{q}_{3}\rceil with a3a_{3} assigned last by AA. Then A~\tilde{A} results in the final apportionment with a3=⌊q~3⌋a_{3}=\lfloor\tilde{q}_{3}\rfloor, which is a not lower quota violation.

Thus lower quota violations occur precisely for d∈I1=[0,d∗)d\in I_{1}=[0,d^{*}). ∎

We summarize the results of this lemma with the below table:

Interval AA assigns a2a_{2} AA assigns a3a_{3} Assigned Last Lower Quota Violation?
I1=[0,d∗)I_{1}=[0,d^{*}) ⌈q~2⌉\lceil\tilde{q}_{2}\rceil ⌊q~3⌋\lfloor\tilde{q}_{3}\rfloor a3a_{3} Yes
I2=[d∗,d∗∗)I_{2}=[d^{*},d^{**}) ⌈q~2⌉\lceil\tilde{q}_{2}\rceil ⌊q~3⌋\lfloor\tilde{q}_{3}\rfloor a2a_{2} No
I3=[d∗∗,1)I_{3}=[d^{**},1) ⌊q~2⌋\lfloor\tilde{q}_{2}\rfloor ⌈q~3⌉\lceil\tilde{q}_{3}\rceil a3a_{3} No
Remark 5.6.

It may occur that d∗=0d^{*}=0, in which case I1=∅I_{1}=\emptyset. It may also occur that d∗∗=1d^{**}=1, in which case I3=∅I_{3}=\emptyset.

Remark 5.7.

By Theorem 3.6, there is no value of d∈(0,1)d\in(0,1) for which the final seat is assigned to state 2 while a2=⌊q~2⌋a_{2}=\lfloor\tilde{q}_{2}\rfloor.

In summary, for each fixed q~3\tilde{q}_{3}, a lower quota violation occurs if and only if d∈[0,d∗).d\in[0,d^{*}). Equivalently, violations occur precisely when q~3\tilde{q}_{3} lies in the interval (⌊q~3⌋,⌊q~3⌋+d∗),(\lfloor\tilde{q}_{3}\rfloor,\;\lfloor\tilde{q}_{3}\rfloor+d^{*}), and these hold within each fixed integer interval.

5.2.1 A formula for d∗d^{*}

To find the interval length ending at d∗d^{*}, a function is required that determines d∗d^{*} given ⌊q~3⌋\lfloor\tilde{q}_{3}\rfloor.

Definition/Lemma 5.8.

There exists a function D​(x)D(x) that calculates d∗d^{*}.

Proof.

By the interval definitions in Lemma 5.5, the priority value associated with the larger state’s final seat is less than the priority value associated with the smaller state’s final seat for d<d∗d<d^{*}. Therefore, d∗d^{*} occurs precisely when the priority values are equal:

⌊q~3⌋+d∗δ​(⌊q~3⌋−1)=M−⌊q~3⌋−d∗δ​(M−1−⌊q~3⌋)\frac{\lfloor\tilde{q}_{3}\rfloor+d^{*}}{\delta(\lfloor\tilde{q}_{3}\rfloor-1)}=\frac{M-\lfloor\tilde{q}_{3}\rfloor-d^{*}}{\delta(M-1-\lfloor\tilde{q}_{3}\rfloor)}

Solving for d∗d^{*} yields:

d∗=(M−⌊q~3⌋)​δ​(⌊q~3⌋−1)−⌊q~3⌋​δ​(M−⌊q~3⌋−1)δ​(⌊q~2⌋−1)+δ​(M−⌊q~2⌋−1)d^{*}=\frac{(M-\lfloor\tilde{q}_{3}\rfloor)\delta(\lfloor\tilde{q}_{3}\rfloor-1)-\lfloor\tilde{q}_{3}\rfloor\delta(M-\lfloor\tilde{q}_{3}\rfloor-1)}{\delta(\lfloor\tilde{q}_{2}\rfloor-1)+\delta(M-\lfloor\tilde{q}_{2}\rfloor-1)}

Since divisor functions satisfy δ​(k)>0\delta(k)>0 for all admissible kk, the denominator is strictly positive, and d∗∈[0,1)d^{*}\in[0,1).

This then lets us define the function D​(k):ℕ→ℝD(k):\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{R}, for MM and δ​(x)\delta(x) given, by

D​(k):=(M−k)​δ​(k−1)−k​δ​(M−k−1)δ​(k−1)+δ​(M−k−1)D(k):=\frac{(M-k)\delta(k-1)-k\delta(M-k-1)}{\delta(k-1)+\delta(M-k-1)}

∎

Remark 5.9.

Note, while D​(k)D(k) is defined for any k∈ℝk\in\mathbb{R} where δ​(k)\delta(k) is defined and the denominator is not zero, we will only need it for positive integers kk. In addition, D​(k)D(k) can output negative values. d∗d^{*} will only be defined by D​(k)D(k) when D​(k)∈[0,1).D(k)\in[0,1). If D​(k)<0,I1=∅.D(k)<0,I_{1}=\emptyset.

Lemma 5.10.

Suppose k=⌊M2⌋k=\left\lfloor\frac{M}{2}\right\rfloor. If MM is even, then d∗=0d^{*}=0 and I1={0}I_{1}=\{0\}. If MM is odd, then d∗<0d^{*}<0 and I1=∅I_{1}=\emptyset. In either case, len​(I1)=0\mathrm{len}(I_{1})=0.

Proof.

For MM even, ⌊M2⌋=M2\lfloor\frac{M}{2}\rfloor=\frac{M}{2}. Then, use the function DD to calculate d∗d^{*}:

d∗=D​(M2)=(M−M2)​δ​(M2−1)−M2​δ​(M−M2−1)δ​(M2−1)+δ​(M−M2−1).d^{*}=D\left(\frac{M}{2}\right)=\frac{\left(M-\frac{M}{2}\right)\delta\left(\frac{M}{2}-1\right)-\frac{M}{2}\delta\left(M-\frac{M}{2}-1\right)}{\delta\left(\frac{M}{2}-1\right)+\delta\left(M-\frac{M}{2}-1\right)}.

Note the numerator is 0 and the denominator is 2​δ​(M2−1)2\delta(\frac{M}{2}-1) is strictly positive, so d∗=0d^{*}=0 Thus, I1=[0,d∗)=∅I_{1}=[0,d^{*})=\emptyset and len​(I1)=0\text{len}(I_{1})=0.

For MM odd, k=⌊M2⌋=M−12k=\left\lfloor\frac{M}{2}\right\rfloor=\frac{M-1}{2}. Then d∗=D​(k).d^{*}=D(k). Since

D​(k)=(M−k)​δ​(k−1)−k​δ​(M−k−1)δ​(k−1)+δ​(M−k−1),D(k)=\frac{(M-k)\delta(k-1)-k\,\delta(M-k-1)}{\delta(k-1)+\delta(M-k-1)},

and since M−k=M+12M-k=\frac{M+1}{2},and M−k−1=kM-k-1=k this simplifies to:

D​(k)=M+12​δ​(k−1)−M−12​δ​(k)δ​(k−1)+δ​(k).D(k)=\frac{\frac{M+1}{2}\,\delta(k-1)-\frac{M-1}{2}\,\delta(k)}{\delta(k-1)+\delta(k)}.

The denominator is strictly positive so the sign of D​(k)D(k) is determined by the numerator N:=M+12​δ​(k−1)−M−12​δ​(k).N:=\frac{M+1}{2}\,\delta(k-1)-\frac{M-1}{2}\,\delta(k).

A direct substitution of the divisor functions yields:

  1. 1.

    For Adams (δ​(s)=s\delta(s)=s), N=−1<0N=-1<0.

  2. 2.

    For Jefferson (δ​(s)=s+1\delta(s)=s+1), N=0N=0.

  3. 3.

    For Webster (δ​(s)=s+12\delta(s)=s+\tfrac{1}{2}), N=−12<0N=-\tfrac{1}{2}<0.

  4. 4.

    For Huntington-Hill and Dean, a straightforward computation likewise gives N<0N<0.

Thus D​(k)≤0D(k)\leq 0 for all five workable methods, with equality occurring only for Jefferson. As such, d∗≤0d^{*}\leq 0. Since admissible d∈[0,1)d\in[0,1), it follows that I1=[0,d∗)=∅I_{1}=[0,d^{*})=\emptyset for d∗≤0d^{*}\leq 0 and therefore len​(I1)=0\mathrm{len}(I_{1})=0. ∎

Remark 5.11.

If ⌊q~2⌋=M\left\lfloor\tilde{q}_{2}\right\rfloor=M, then the associated lower-violation interval I1I_{1} has length zero since D​(M)=0D(M)=0. If k<⌈M2⌉k<\lceil\frac{M}{2}\rceil, then D​(k)<0D(k)<0 and I1=∅.I_{1}=\emptyset. Thus, when searching for quota violations, it suffices to consider ⌊q~2⌋∈[⌈M2⌉,M−1]\left\lfloor\tilde{q}_{2}\right\rfloor\in[\left\lceil\frac{M}{2}\right\rceil,\,M-1].

We are now ready to prove the main result of this section:

Theorem 5.12.

Let AA be a divisor method with divisor function δ\delta that guarantees nonzero allocations on M seats and n=3n=3 states. Then, under the asymptotic uniform distribution on reduced population vectors (1,x,y)(1,x,y), with 1<x<y1<x<y, the probability of a quota violation is the same as the probability of a quota violation caused by states guaranteed nonzero allocations and is calculated by:

P​(A​(1,x,y)​ has a q.v.)=∑k=⌈M/2⌉M−1M​(1k−1k+D​(k))P(A(1,x,y)\text{ has a q.v.})=\sum_{k=\lceil M/2\rceil}^{M-1}M\left(\frac{1}{k}-\frac{1}{k+D(k)}\right)

where kk indexes the integer values of ⌊q~3⌋\lfloor\tilde{q}_{3}\rfloor, where

q~3=2​M3−3​τ,τ=mean​(1,x,y)−median​(1,x,y)max⁡(1,x,y)=1−2​x+y3​y.\tilde{q}_{3}=\frac{2M}{3-3\tau},\qquad\tau=\frac{\mathrm{mean}(1,x,y)-\mathrm{median}(1,x,y)}{\max(1,x,y)}=\dfrac{1-2x+y}{3y}.

and

D​(k)=(M−k)​δ​(k−1)−k​δ​(M−k−1)δ​(k−1)+δ​(M−k−1).D(k)=\frac{(M-k)\delta(k-1)-k\,\delta(M-k-1)}{\delta(k-1)+\delta(M-k-1)}.
Proof.

Combining the previous lemmas yields the stated expression. By Theorem 5.3 it suffices to find the probability that a given τ\tau is ultimately violatory. Since q~3=2​M3−3​τ\tilde{q}_{3}=\frac{2M}{3-3\tau} is a bijection, fixing τ\tau then determines q~3\tilde{q}_{3}, and vice versa.

By Lemma 5.5, for a fixed q~3\tilde{q}_{3}, quota violations can only be lower quota violations and they only occur in the interval (⌊q~3⌋,⌊q~3⌋+d∗)(\lfloor\tilde{q}_{3}\rfloor,\lfloor\tilde{q}_{3}\rfloor+d^{*}).

Translate this interval into its corresponding values of τ\tau through the strictly increasing relation τ=1−2​M3​q~3\tau=1-\frac{2M}{3\tilde{q}_{3}} to map this interval to

(1−2​M3​⌊q~3⌋,1−2​M3​(⌊q~3⌋+D​(⌊q~3⌋))),(1-\frac{2M}{3\lfloor\tilde{q}_{3}\rfloor},1-\frac{2M}{3(\lfloor\tilde{q}_{3}\rfloor+D(\lfloor\tilde{q}_{3}\rfloor))}),

where D​(x)D(x) calculates d∗d^{*} as defined in Lemma 5.8.

The probability of an ultimately-violatory τ\tau is then the sum of the length of all such intervals for all choices of q~3∈{⌈M2⌉,…,M−1}\tilde{q}_{3}\in\left\{\left\lceil\frac{M}{2}\right\rceil,\dots,M-1\right\} by Lemma 5.5 and Remark 5.11 over the length of all possible τ\tau values, which is 23\frac{2}{3} by Lemma 4.4.

The probability is then:

32​(∑⌊q~3⌋=⌈M2⌉M−1(1−2​M3​(⌊q~3⌋+D​(⌊q~3⌋)))−(1−2​M3​⌊q~3⌋))\frac{3}{2}\left(\sum_{\lfloor\tilde{q}_{3}\rfloor=\lceil\frac{M}{2}\rceil}^{M-1}\left(1-\frac{2M}{3(\lfloor\tilde{q}_{3}\rfloor+D(\lfloor\tilde{q}_{3}\rfloor))}\right)-\left(1-\frac{2M}{3\lfloor\tilde{q}_{3}\rfloor}\right)\right)

which simplifies to

∑⌊q~3⌋=⌈M2⌉M−1M​(1⌊q~3⌋−1⌊q~3⌋+D​(⌊q~3⌋)).\sum_{\lfloor\tilde{q}_{3}\rfloor=\lceil\frac{M}{2}\rceil}^{M-1}M\left(\frac{1}{\lfloor\tilde{q}_{3}\rfloor}-\frac{1}{\lfloor\tilde{q}_{3}\rfloor+D(\lfloor\tilde{q}_{3}\rfloor)}\right).

∎

This formula has a nice closed form for the Modified Jefferson’s method:

Corollary 5.12.1.

Let AA be the Modified Jefferson method with divisor function

δ​(s)={0s=0,s+1s≥1.\delta(s)=\begin{cases}0&s=0,\\ s+1&s\geq 1.\end{cases}

Then, under the asymptotic uniform distribution on reduced population vectors (1,x,y)(1,x,y) with 1<x<y1<x<y, the probability of a quota violation caused by nonzero allocations is exactly

PMJEFF=1M−1.P_{M}^{\mathrm{JEFF}}=\frac{1}{M-1}.
Proof.

By Theorem 5.12, the probability of a quota violation is

PMJEFF=∑k=⌈M/2⌉M−1M​(1k−1k+D​(k)),P_{M}^{\mathrm{JEFF}}=\sum_{k=\lceil M/2\rceil}^{M-1}M\left(\frac{1}{k}-\frac{1}{k+D(k)}\right),

where

D​(k)=(M−k)​δ​(k−1)−k​δ​(M−k−1)δ​(k−1)+δ​(M−k−1).D(k)=\frac{(M-k)\,\delta(k-1)-k\,\delta(M-k-1)}{\delta(k-1)+\delta(M-k-1)}.

Since δ​(k)=k+1\delta(k)=k+1 for k≥1k\geq 1, δ​(k−1)=k\delta(k-1)=k for all k≥1k\geq 1. For k≤M−2k\leq M-2, M−k−1≥1M-k-1\geq 1, so δ​(M−k−1)=M−k\delta(M-k-1)=M-k. Substituting:

D​(k)=(M−k)⋅k−k⋅(M−k)k+(M−k)=0M=0,D(k)=\frac{(M-k)\cdot k-k\cdot(M-k)}{k+(M-k)}=\frac{0}{M}=0,

so every summand vanishes for ⌈M/2⌉≤k≤M−2\lceil M/2\rceil\leq k\leq M-2.

For k=M−1k=M-1, M−k−1=0M-k-1=0 and the modified case δ​(0)=0\delta(0)=0 applies. Substituting:

D​(M−1)=1⋅(M−1)−(M−1)⋅0(M−1)+0=M−1M−1=1.D(M-1)=\frac{1\cdot(M-1)-(M-1)\cdot 0}{(M-1)+0}=\frac{M-1}{M-1}=1.

Therefore k+D​(k)=Mk+D(k)=M, and the summand at k=M−1k=M-1 is:

M​(1M−1−1M)=M⋅1M​(M−1)=1M−1.M\left(\frac{1}{M-1}-\frac{1}{M}\right)=M\cdot\frac{1}{M(M-1)}=\frac{1}{M-1}.

Since all other summands are zero, the total probability is

PMJEFF=1M−1,P_{M}^{\mathrm{JEFF}}=\frac{1}{M-1},

which confirms the value stated in Section 5.4 and agrees with limM→∞PMJEFF=0\lim_{M\to\infty}P_{M}^{\mathrm{JEFF}}=0 as given in Theorem 5.16. ∎

We would now like to lift this result from the reduced population vector (1,x,y)(1,x,y) to the original population vector (p1,p2,p3)(p_{1},p_{2},p_{3}). Apportionments are proportional, so events like lower quota violations are invariant under scaling, but probabilities are not unless the sampling model is scale invariant. A natural choice then is to sample (p1,p2,p3)(p_{1},p_{2},p_{3}) from a scale-invariant distribution, e.g. as i.i.d. continuous variables with density depending only on their ratios. Proposition 5.2 gives the assumption needed: that p2p3\frac{p_{2}}{p_{3}} be uniform on (0,1)(0,1). Then τ\tau will be uniform on (−13,13)(-\frac{1}{3},\frac{1}{3}) and we can lift the probability formulas. Without these assumptions on (p1,p2,p3)(p_{1},p_{2},p_{3}), the probability results from the prior theorem may no longer apply. We formalize this in the following.

Corollary 5.12.2.

Let (p1,p2,p3)(p_{1},p_{2},p_{3}) be a random population vector with p1<p2<p3p_{1}<p_{2}<p_{3}, and define x=p2p1x=\frac{p_{2}}{p_{1}}, y=p3p1y=\frac{p_{3}}{p_{1}}. Assume that the induced ratios satisfy the hypotheses of Proposition 5.2; namely, that u=xy=p2p3u=\frac{x}{y}=\frac{p_{2}}{p_{3}} is asymptotically uniformly distributed on (0,1)(0,1) and y→∞y\to\infty in density.

Let AA be a divisor method that guarantees nonzero allocations, and let τ\tau be as in Definition 4.1. Then:

  1. 1.

    τ∼Unif​(−13,13)\tau\sim\mathrm{Unif}\!\left(-\frac{1}{3},\frac{1}{3}\right) asymptotically.

  2. 2.

    The probability that A​(p1,p2,p3)A(p_{1},p_{2},p_{3}) exhibits a lower quota violation caused by nonzero allocations satisfies

    P​(quota violation)=P​(quota violation caused by nonzero allocation)=P​(τ​ is ultimately violatory).P(\text{quota violation})=P(\text{quota violation caused by nonzero allocation})=P(\tau\text{ is ultimately violatory}).

In particular, this probability coincides with that obtained under the asymptotic uniform distribution on reduced population vectors (1,x,y)(1,x,y).

Proof.

By Lemma 3.1 (Proportionality), divisor methods are invariant under scaling, so A​(p1,p2,p3)=A​(1,x,y).A(p_{1},p_{2},p_{3})=A(1,x,y). Thus quota violation events depend only on the reduced population vector. By Proposition 5.2, the assumptions on (p1,p2,p3)(p_{1},p_{2},p_{3}) imply that the induced distribution of τ\tau converges to Unif​(−13,13)\mathrm{Unif}\!\left(-\frac{1}{3},\frac{1}{3}\right).

By Theorem 4.6, for each τ\tau outside a finite exceptional set, the occurrence or non-occurrence of a lower quota violation for A​(1,x,y​(x,τ))A(1,x,y(x,\tau)) stabilizes for all sufficiently large xx, and is completely determined by whether τ\tau is ultimately violatory. Therefore, the probability of a lower quota violation given τ\tau depends only on τ\tau. As such, P​(lower quota violation)=P​(τ​ is ultimately violatory).P(\text{lower quota violation})=P(\tau\text{ is ultimately violatory}).

Since both the present model on (p1,p2,p3)(p_{1},p_{2},p_{3}) and the asymptotic uniform model on (1,x,y)(1,x,y) induce the same limiting distribution on τ\tau, the resulting probabilities coincide. ∎

5.3 Numerical Behavior of the Probability Formula

Table 1 below presents approximate values of the probability of lower quota violations caused by nonzero allocations for the classical divisor methods and increasing values of MM. Values are rounded to 3 decimal places.

Several patterns emerge:

  1. 1.

    The probabilities appear to converge as M→∞M\to\infty to method-dependent constants. The next section will prove this to be true.

  2. 2.

    Since the Jefferson method has no lower quota violations, the only quota violations that occur will arise from the modified method. As such, the sum PMP_{M} in Theorem 5.12 above gives the probability for all lower quota violations for three states. This formula simplifies to 1M−1\frac{1}{M-1} by Corollary 5.12.1. This shows that as MM increases, the limit of the probability for a lower quota violation approaches 0.

  3. 3.

    Adams method exhibits limiting behavior quickly near 0.386. This value turns out to be exactly 2​ln⁡(2)−1≈0.3862942\ln(2)-1\approx 0.386294 as shown in the next section.

  4. 4.

    The Webster, Huntington-Hill, and Dean’s method all exhibit limiting behavior, with limiting values near 0.1930.193. This limit will turn out to be ln⁡(2)−12≈0.193147\ln(2)-\tfrac{1}{2}\approx 0.193147 as shown in the next section.

Remark 5.13.

The probabilities in Table 1 for the Modified Webster method admit a particularly clean interpretation. By Theorem 4.5(b) of [13], Webster’s method is the unique divisor method for which no quota violations occur in the three-state case when there are no allocation constraints. Therefore, every quota violation appearing in Table 1 for Modified Webster is entirely attributable to the nonzero allocation constraint. In this sense, Modified Webster isolates the effect of the constraint most cleanly among the five workable methods: its unmodified violation probability is exactly zero, making the values in Table 1 a pure measure of the constraint’s impact. By contrast, the Adams, Dean, and Huntington-Hill methods have nonzero unconstrained violation probabilities for n=3n=3 states, so their entries reflect a combination of the method’s inherent tendency to violate quota and the additional effect of the nonzero allocation constraint.

5.4 Asymptotic Limit as M→∞M\to\infty

We now derive a closed-form expression for the limiting probability of lower quota violations caused by nonzero allocations in n=3n=3 seats for different apportionment methods as M→∞M\to\infty.

By Corollary 5.12.1, the Modified Jefferson’s method has PM=1M+1P_{M}=\frac{1}{M+1} and therefore

limM→∞PMJEFF=0.\lim_{M\to\infty}P_{M}^{\mathrm{JEFF}}=0.

Next, we analyze Adams Method as M→∞M\to\infty.

Theorem 5.14 (Limiting Probability for Adams Method).

Let AA be the Adams apportionment method with divisor function δ​(x)=x\delta(x)=x and let MM denote the total number of seats. Then, under the asymptotic uniform distribution on reduced population vectors (1,x,y)(1,x,y) with 1<x<y1<x<y, the probability of a quota violation converges as M→∞M\to\infty to

P∞ADAMS=limM→∞∑k=⌈M/2⌉M−1M​(1k−1k+D​(k))=2​ln⁡(2)−1≈0.386294,P_{\infty}^{\text{ADAMS}}=\lim_{M\to\infty}\sum_{k=\lceil M/2\rceil}^{M-1}M\Biggl(\frac{1}{k}-\frac{1}{k+D(k)}\Biggr)=2\ln(2)-1\approx 0.386294,

where

D​(k)=(2​k−M)M−2.D(k)=\frac{(2k-M)}{M-2}.
Proof.

When δ​(k)=k\delta(k)=k, D​(k)D(k) from Theorem 5.12 simplifies to D​(k)=2​k−MM−2D(k)=\frac{2k-M}{M-2} . Define the variable x=kM∈[12,1].x=\frac{k}{M}\in\left[\tfrac{1}{2},1\right].

Then, for large MM,

D​(k)=2​k−MM−2∼2​k−MM=2​x−1.D(k)=\frac{2k-M}{M-2}\sim\frac{2k-M}{M}=2x-1.

The summand in PMP_{M} then becomes:

M​(1k−1k+D​(k))\displaystyle M\left(\frac{1}{k}-\frac{1}{k+D(k)}\right) =M​(D​(k)k(k+D(k))\displaystyle=M\left(\frac{D(k)}{k(k+D(k)}\right)
∼M​(2​x−1(M​x)​(M​x+2​x−1))\displaystyle\sim M\left(\frac{2x-1}{(Mx)(Mx+2x-1)}\right)
∼M​(2​x−1(M​x)​(M​x))\displaystyle\sim M\left(\frac{2x-1}{(Mx)(Mx)}\right)
∼2​x−1x2⋅1M\displaystyle\sim\frac{2x-1}{x^{2}}\cdot\frac{1}{M}

As M→∞M\to\infty, the sum over kk becomes an integral over xx, and 1M\frac{1}{M} becomes d​xdx:

∑k=⌈M/2⌉M−1M​(1k−1k+D​(k))∼∫1/21(2​x−1x2)​𝑑x.\sum_{k=\lceil M/2\rceil}^{M-1}M\left(\frac{1}{k}-\frac{1}{k+D(k)}\right)\sim\int_{1/2}^{1}\left(\frac{2x-1}{x^{2}}\right)dx.

Evaluate the integral to get

P∞ADAMS=2​ln⁡(2)−1≈0.386294.P_{\infty}^{\text{ADAMS}}=2\ln(2)-1\approx 0.386294.

∎

The next result identifies a common asymptotic behavior for a broad class of divisor methods whose divisor functions differ from linear growth by a bounded perturbation. In particular, the theorem shows that the limiting probability of a quota violation depends only on the first-order structure of the divisor function, leading to a common limit shared by several classical methods.

Theorem 5.15 (Limiting Probability for Asymptotically Linear Divisor Methods).

Let AA be a divisor method with divisor function satisfying

δ​(k)=k+12+O​(1k).\delta(k)=k+\frac{1}{2}+O\!\left(\frac{1}{k}\right).

Let

PM=∑k=⌈M/2⌉M−1M​(1k−1k+D​(k)),P_{M}=\sum_{k=\lceil M/2\rceil}^{M-1}M\left(\frac{1}{k}-\frac{1}{k+D(k)}\right),

where

D​(k)=(M−k)​δ​(k−1)−k​δ​(M−k−1)δ​(k−1)+δ​(M−k−1).D(k)=\frac{(M-k)\delta(k-1)-k\,\delta(M-k-1)}{\delta(k-1)+\delta(M-k-1)}.

Then

limM→∞PM=ln⁡2−12.\lim_{M\to\infty}P_{M}=\ln 2-\frac{1}{2}.

In particular, this holds for the Huntington-Hill, Modified Webster, and Dean method.

Proof.

For the Huntington-Hill method, the divisor function δ​(k)=k​(k+1)\delta(k)=\sqrt{k(k+1)} has Laurent expansion k+12−18​k+…k+\frac{1}{2}-\frac{1}{8k}+\ldots. After polynomial division, the divisor function for Dean’s method is δ​(k)=2​k​(k+1)2​k+1=k+12−12(2k+1\delta(k)=\frac{2k(k+1)}{2k+1}=k+\frac{1}{2}-\frac{1}{2(2k+1} . Along with the divisor function for Webster’s method, δ​(k)=k+0.5\delta(k)=k+0.5, all three apportionment methods satisfy the assumption of the theorem.

Using the assumption δ​(k)∼k+12\delta(k)\sim k+\frac{1}{2}, write

δ​(k−1)=k−12δ​(M−k−1)=(M−k)−12.\delta(k-1)=k-\frac{1}{2}\qquad\delta(M-k-1)=(M-k)-\frac{1}{2}.

Substitute into D​(k)D(k) to get

D​(k)=(M−k)​(k−12)−k​((M−k)−12)(k−12)+((M−k)−12)=2​k−M2​(M−1)D(k)=\frac{(M-k)(k-\frac{1}{2})-k((M-k)-\frac{1}{2})}{(k-\frac{1}{2})+((M-k)-\frac{1}{2})}=\frac{2k-M}{2(M-1)}

Since D​(k)<1D(k)<1 and k>1,D​(k)k<1k>1,\frac{D(k)}{k}<1, by the geometric series,

1k+D​(k)=1k⋅11+D​(k)k=1k​(1−D​(k)k+(D​(k)k)2+…)=1k−D​(k)k2+…\frac{1}{k+D(k)}=\frac{1}{k}\cdot\frac{1}{1+\frac{D(k)}{k}}=\frac{1}{k}\left(1-\frac{D(k)}{k}+\left(\frac{D(k)}{k}\right)^{2}+\ldots\right)=\frac{1}{k}-\frac{D(k)}{k^{2}}+\ldots

where the remaining terms are of order 1M3\frac{1}{M^{3}}. The expression in the summand of PMP_{M} then becomes

M​(1k−1k+D​(k))∼M​(1k−(1k−D​(k)k2))=M⋅D​(k)k2M\left(\frac{1}{k}-\frac{1}{k+D(k)}\right)\sim M\left(\frac{1}{k}-\left(\frac{1}{k}-\frac{D(k)}{k^{2}}\right)\right)=M\cdot\frac{D(k)}{k^{2}}

Set x=kM∈[12,1]x=\frac{k}{M}\in[\tfrac{1}{2},1]. Then as M→∞,D​(k)∼2​x−12M\to\infty,D(k)\sim\frac{2x-1}{2}. Then

M​(1k−1k+D​(k))∼M⋅D​(k)k2∼M​(2​x−1)x2​M2=2​x−1x2⋅1MM\left(\frac{1}{k}-\frac{1}{k+D(k)}\right)\sim M\cdot\frac{D(k)}{k^{2}}\sim M\frac{(2x-1)}{x^{2}M^{2}}=\frac{2x-1}{x^{2}}\cdot\frac{1}{M}

The sum is a Riemann sum, so as M→∞,M\to\infty,

PM→∫1/212​x−12​x2​𝑑x.P_{M}\to\int_{1/2}^{1}\frac{2x-1}{2x^{2}}\,dx.

Evaluating this integral gives

P∞HH=P∞DEAN=P∞WEB=ln⁡2−12≈0.1932.P_{\infty}^{\text{HH}}=P_{\infty}^{\text{DEAN}}=P_{\infty}^{\text{WEB}}=\ln 2-\frac{1}{2}\approx 0.1932.

∎

Summarizing the results of this section:

Theorem 5.16.

Under the asymptotic uniform distribution for vectors (1,x,y)(1,x,y) with 1<x<y1<x<y, we have as M→∞M\to\infty that the probability of a quota violation caused by nonzero allocations converges to 2​ln⁡(2)−1≈0.3862942\ln(2)-1\approx 0.386294 in the Adams method, to 0 in the Modified Jefferson method, and to ln⁡(2)−12≈0.1932\ln(2)-\frac{1}{2}\approx 0.1932 in the Huntington-Hill, Modified Webster, and Dean methods.

5.5 Probability of Quota Violations along τ\tau-Lines

Next, for divisor methods AA corresponding to Huntington-Hill, modified Webster, modified Jefferson, or Dean, extend the results of the previous section to characterize when quota violations occur due to a guaranteed seat.

To do this, the first step is to recall the threshold xτFx_{\tau}^{F} that quantifies when the asymptotic behavior in Theorem 4.7 effectively holds. Specifically, for x>xτFx>x_{\tau}^{F}, the guaranteed-seat state causes floor stabilization and potential quota violations. Equivalently, xτFx_{\tau}^{F} (from Theorem 4.7) is the infimum of all zz such that for all x>zx>z, the following floor conditions hold (i.e., the floors stabilize).

  • •

    ⌊M​(1−3​τ)(3−3​τ)​x−3​τ⌋=0\left\lfloor\frac{M(1-3\tau)}{(3-3\tau)x-3\tau}\right\rfloor=0

  • •

    ⌊M​(1−3​τ)​x(3−3​τ)​x−3​τ⌋=⌊M​(1−3​τ)3−3​τ⌋\left\lfloor\frac{M(1-3\tau)x}{(3-3\tau)x-3\tau}\right\rfloor=\left\lfloor\frac{M(1-3\tau)}{3-3\tau}\right\rfloor

  • •

    ⌊2​M​(1−3​τ)​x−M+3​M​τ(3−3​τ)​(1−3​τ)​x−3​τ​(1−3​τ)⌋=⌊2​M3−3​τ⌋.\left\lfloor\frac{2M(1-3\tau)x-M+3M\tau}{(3-3\tau)(1-3\tau)x-3\tau(1-3\tau)}\right\rfloor=\left\lfloor\frac{2M}{3-3\tau}\right\rfloor.

By Theorem 4.7, for such xx, the apportionment converges to A~​(q~2,q~3)\tilde{A}(\tilde{q}_{2},\tilde{q}_{3}), with xτx_{\tau} being the infinum over all z>xτFz>x_{\tau}^{F} such that A~​(q~2,q~3)=A​(1,z,y​(z,τ))\tilde{A}(\tilde{q}_{2},\tilde{q}_{3})=A(1,z,y(z,\tau)).

On a τ\tau-line, parametrized in y​(x,τ)y(x,\tau)

y​(x,τ)=2​x−11−3​τ=21−3​τ​x−11−3​τy(x,\tau)=\frac{2x-1}{1-3\tau}=\frac{2}{1-3\tau}x-\frac{1}{1-3\tau}

Define then for a fixed τ\tau,

yτ=y​(xτ,τ)andyτF=y​(xτF,τ)y_{\tau}=y(x_{\tau},\tau)\qquad\text{and}\qquad y_{\tau}^{F}=y(x_{\tau}^{F},\tau)

Also recall along a τ\tau-line, the reduced population (1,x,y)(1,x,y) can be parametrized as (1,x​(y,τ),y)(1,x(y,\tau),y), where x​(y,τ)=12​y−32​τ​y+12,x(y,\tau)=\frac{1}{2}y-\frac{3}{2}\tau y+\frac{1}{2}, so that the total population is 1+x​(y,τ)+y=3−3​τ2​y+321+x(y,\tau)+y=\frac{3-3\tau}{2}y+\frac{3}{2}. Then the standard quotas, as functions of yy, are:

q1​(y)=M1+x​(y,τ)+y,q2​(y)=M​x​(y,τ)1+x​(y,τ)+y,q3​(y)=M​y1+x​(y,τ)+y.q_{1}(y)=\frac{M}{1+x(y,\tau)+y},q_{2}(y)=\frac{Mx(y,\tau)}{1+x(y,\tau)+y},q_{3}(y)=\frac{My}{1+x(y,\tau)+y}.

Next, determine yτFy_{\tau}^{F}, the smallest value of yy such that the floor stabilization conditions hold along the τ\tau-line.

Definition/Lemma 5.17 (Guaranteed-Seat Threshold).

Fix τ∈(−13,13)\tau\in(-\tfrac{1}{3},\tfrac{1}{3}). There exists a constant yτF>0y_{\tau}^{F}>0 such that for all y>yτFy>y_{\tau}^{F}, the floors of the standard quotas of the distribution (1,x​(y,τ),y)(1,x(y,\tau),y) stabilize:

(⌊q1​(y)⌋,⌊q2​(y)⌋,⌊q3​(y)⌋)=(0,⌊q~2⌋,⌊q~3⌋),\bigl(\lfloor q_{1}(y)\rfloor,\lfloor q_{2}(y)\rfloor,\lfloor q_{3}(y)\rfloor\bigr)=\bigl(0,\lfloor\tilde{q}_{2}\rfloor,\lfloor\tilde{q}_{3}\rfloor\bigr),

where

q~2=M​(1−3​τ)3−3​τ,q~3=2​M3−3​τ.\tilde{q}_{2}=\frac{M(1-3\tau)}{3-3\tau},\qquad\tilde{q}_{3}=\frac{2M}{3-3\tau}.
Proof.

Analyze each quota separately. For state 1,

q1​(y)=M3−3​τ2​y+32.q_{1}(y)=\frac{M}{\frac{3-3\tau}{2}y+\frac{3}{2}}.

This is strictly decreasing in yy and satisfies q1​(y)→0q_{1}(y)\to 0 as y→∞y\to\infty. Then there exists y1​(τ)y_{1}(\tau) such that for all y>y1​(τ)y>y_{1}(\tau), 0<q1​(y)<10<q_{1}(y)<1, so ⌊q1​(y)⌋=0\lfloor q_{1}(y)\rfloor=0.

For state 3,

q3​(y)=M​y3−3​τ2​y+32=2​M3−3​τ+3y.q_{3}(y)=\frac{My}{\frac{3-3\tau}{2}y+\frac{3}{2}}=\frac{2M}{3-3\tau+\frac{3}{y}}.

So q3​(y)q_{3}(y) is strictly increasing and converges to q~3\tilde{q}_{3} as y→∞y\to\infty.

If q~3∈ℤ\tilde{q}_{3}\in\mathbb{Z}, then q3​(y)q_{3}(y) converges monotonically to q~3\tilde{q}_{3}, so there exists y3​(τ)y_{3}(\tau) such that ⌊q3​(y)⌋=q~3−1\lfloor q_{3}(y)\rfloor=\tilde{q}_{3}-1 for all sufficiently large yy.

If q~3∉ℤ\tilde{q}_{3}\notin\mathbb{Z}, choose ϵ>0\epsilon>0 small enough that

(q~3−ε,q~3+ε)⊂(⌊q~3⌋,⌊q~3⌋+1).(\tilde{q}_{3}-\varepsilon,\tilde{q}_{3}+\varepsilon)\subset\bigl(\lfloor\tilde{q}_{3}\rfloor,\lfloor\tilde{q}_{3}\rfloor+1\bigr).

Then there exists y3​(τ)y_{3}(\tau) such that for all y>y3​(τ)y>y_{3}(\tau), |q3​(y)−q~3|<ϵ,|q_{3}(y)-\tilde{q}_{3}|<\epsilon, and as such, ⌊q3​(y)⌋=⌊q~3⌋.\lfloor q_{3}(y)\rfloor=\lfloor\tilde{q}_{3}\rfloor.

For state 2, use x​(y,τ)x(y,\tau):

q2​(y)=M​x​(y,τ)3−3​τ2​y+32=M​(12​y−32​τ​y+12)3−3​τ2​y+32.q_{2}(y)=\frac{Mx(y,\tau)}{\frac{3-3\tau}{2}y+\frac{3}{2}}=\frac{M\left(\frac{1}{2}y-\frac{3}{2}\tau y+\frac{1}{2}\right)}{\frac{3-3\tau}{2}y+\frac{3}{2}}.

A direct computation shows that q2′​(y)q^{\prime}_{2}(y) has constant sign depending on τ\tau:

q2′​(y)​{>0if ​τ<0,=0if ​τ=0,<0if ​τ>0,q_{2}^{\prime}(y)\begin{cases}>0&\text{if }\tau<0,\\ =0&\text{if }\tau=0,\\ <0&\text{if }\tau>0,\end{cases}

so q2​(y)q_{2}(y) is monotone and converges to q~2\tilde{q}_{2} as y→∞y\to\infty.

Thus, as for state 3, there exists y2​(τ)y_{2}(\tau) such that for all y>y2​(τ)y>y_{2}(\tau), ⌊q2​(y)⌋=⌊q~2⌋.\lfloor q_{2}(y)\rfloor=\lfloor\tilde{q}_{2}\rfloor. Finally, define

yτF:=max⁡{y1​(τ),y2​(τ),y3​(τ)}.y_{\tau}^{F}:=\max\{y_{1}(\tau),y_{2}(\tau),y_{3}(\tau)\}.

Then for all y>yτFy>y_{\tau}^{F},

(⌊q1​(y)⌋,⌊q2​(y)⌋,⌊q3​(y)⌋)=(0,⌊q~2⌋,⌊q~3⌋).(\lfloor q_{1}(y)\rfloor,\lfloor q_{2}(y)\rfloor,\lfloor q_{3}(y)\rfloor)=\left(0,\lfloor\tilde{q}_{2}\rfloor,\lfloor\tilde{q}_{3}\rfloor\right).

Additionally, a direct computation grants:

y1​(τ)\displaystyle y_{1}(\tau) =2​M−33−3​τ\displaystyle=\frac{2M-3}{3-3\tau}
y2​(τ)\displaystyle y_{2}(\tau) ={3​⌊q~2⌋−MM−3​M​τ−3​⌊q~2⌋+3​τ​⌊q~2⌋τ<01τ=03​(⌊q~2⌋+1)−MM−3​M​τ−3​(⌊q~2⌋+1)+3​τ​(⌊q~2⌋+1)τ>0\displaystyle=\left\{\begin{array}[]{lcl}\frac{3\lfloor\tilde{q}_{2}\rfloor-M}{M-3M\tau-3\lfloor\tilde{q}_{2}\rfloor+3\tau\lfloor\tilde{q}_{2}\rfloor}&\tau<0\\ 1&\tau=0\\ \frac{3(\lfloor\tilde{q}_{2}\rfloor+1)-M}{M-3M\tau-3(\lfloor\tilde{q}_{2}\rfloor+1)+3\tau(\lfloor\tilde{q}_{2}\rfloor+1)}&\tau>0\end{array}\right.
y3​(τ)\displaystyle y_{3}(\tau) =3​⌊q~3⌋2​M−3​⌊q~3⌋+3​τ​⌊q~3⌋.\displaystyle=\frac{3\lfloor\tilde{q}_{3}\rfloor}{2M-3\lfloor\tilde{q}_{3}\rfloor+3\tau\lfloor\tilde{q}_{3}\rfloor}.

∎

Note by definition and construction, yτF≤yτy_{\tau}^{F}\leq y_{\tau} since yτy_{\tau} imposes more conditions. For a fixed τ\tau, the constant yτFy_{\tau}^{F} identifies the smallest reduced population of the largest state along the τ\tau-line for which the floors of all standard quotas stabilize. When y>yτFy>y_{\tau}^{F}, the guaranteed seat for state 1 could create an apportionment in such a way that state 3 receives fewer seats than its lower quota, producing a quota violation. This observation motivates the following lemma.

Lemma 5.18.

Let τ\tau be ultimately violatory. If y>yτFy>y_{\tau}^{F}, then the apportionment A~1​(1,x​(y,τ),y)\tilde{A}_{1}(1,x(y,\tau),y) produces a quota violation caused by the guaranteed seat of state 1. Equivalently, if τ\tau is ultimately violatory yτF=yτy_{\tau}^{F}=y_{\tau}

Proof.

For y>yτFy>y_{\tau}^{F}, by Lemma 5.17,

(⌊q1⌋,⌊q2⌋,⌊q3⌋)=(0,⌊q~2⌋,⌊q~3⌋).(\lfloor q_{1}\rfloor,\lfloor q_{2}\rfloor,\lfloor q_{3}\rfloor)=(0,\lfloor\tilde{q}_{2}\rfloor,\lfloor\tilde{q}_{3}\rfloor).

Since state 1 is guaranteed a seat, by Theorem 3.6 the only possible apportionments are either (1,⌊q~2⌋,⌊q~3⌋)(1,\lfloor\tilde{q}_{2}\rfloor,\lfloor\tilde{q}_{3}\rfloor) or (1,⌊q~2⌋+1,⌊q~3⌋−1)(1,\lfloor\tilde{q}_{2}\rfloor+1,\lfloor\tilde{q}_{3}\rfloor-1). Since τ\tau is ultimately violatory, the final apportionment must be (1,⌊q~2⌋+1,⌊q~3⌋−1)(1,\lfloor\tilde{q}_{2}\rfloor+1,\lfloor\tilde{q}_{3}\rfloor-1). This means that either the apportionment where y>yτFy>y_{\tau}^{F} is either always (1,⌊q~2⌋+1,⌊q~3⌋−1)(1,\lfloor\tilde{q}_{2}\rfloor+1,\lfloor\tilde{q}_{3}\rfloor-1), in which case, the Lemma is proved, or is the first apportionment and becomes (1,⌊q~2⌋+1,⌊q~3⌋−1)(1,\lfloor\tilde{q}_{2}\rfloor+1,\lfloor\tilde{q}_{3}\rfloor-1). However, this latter scenario violates population monotonicity on the third state. ∎

Definition/Lemma 5.19 (Non-Violatory Threshold).

Fix τ∈(−13,13)\tau\in(-\frac{1}{3},\frac{1}{3}) that is ultimately non-violatory. There exists a constant yτ>0y_{\tau}>0 such that for all y>yτy>y_{\tau}, the apportionment A~1​(q~2,q~3)\tilde{A}_{1}(\tilde{q}_{2},\tilde{q}_{3}) stabilizes without producing a quota violation.

In particular, yτy_{\tau} is given by

yτ:=max⁡{yτF,yτ∗},y_{\tau}:=\max\{y_{\tau}^{F},y_{\tau}^{*}\},

where yτy_{\tau} is the threshold from Lemma 5.17, and yτ∗y_{\tau}^{*} solves the priority-equality condition

M​yδ​(⌊q~3⌋−1)=M​(12​y−32​τ​y+12)δ​(M−⌊q~3⌋−1).\frac{My}{\delta(\lfloor\tilde{q}_{3}\rfloor-1)}=\frac{M\left(\frac{1}{2}y-\frac{3}{2}\tau y+\frac{1}{2}\right)}{\delta(M-\lfloor\tilde{q}_{3}\rfloor-1)}.
Proof.

First consider the case where yτFy_{\tau}^{F} does not yet calculate yτy_{\tau}. As in the previous proof, the only valid apportionments after yτFy_{\tau}^{F} are (⌈q1⌉,⌈q2⌉,⌊q3⌋−1)(\lceil q_{1}\rceil,\lceil q_{2}\rceil,\lfloor q_{3}\rfloor-1) and (⌈q1⌉,⌊q2⌋,⌊q3⌋)(\lceil q_{1}\rceil,\lfloor q_{2}\rfloor,\lfloor q_{3}\rfloor). Since by assumption the final apportionment is non-violatory, the apportionment must be moving from (⌈q1⌉,⌈q2⌉,⌊q3⌋−1)(\lceil q_{1}\rceil,\lceil q_{2}\rceil,\lfloor q_{3}\rfloor-1) toward (⌈q1⌉,⌊q2⌋,⌊q3⌋).(\lceil q_{1}\rceil,\lfloor q_{2}\rfloor,\lfloor q_{3}\rfloor).

Consider the apportionment functions A​(q~2,q~3)A(\tilde{q}_{2},\tilde{q}_{3}) and A~1​(q~2,q~3)\tilde{A}_{1}(\tilde{q}_{2},\tilde{q}_{3}). Before reaching yτy_{\tau}, state 3 receives its floor (and potentially loses a seat), while state 2 receives its ceiling. This implies that, at that point, the priority value for assigning state 3 an additional seat is less than the priority value for assigning state 2 an additional seat.

As yy increases past yτy_{\tau}, the last seat is assigned in such a way that the priority values equalize, and the inequality flips. Solving for the value of yy where the priority values are equal gives

M​yδ​(⌊q~3⌋−1)=M​(12​y−32​τ​y+12)δ​(⌊q~2⌋).\frac{My}{\delta(\lfloor\tilde{q}_{3}\rfloor-1)}=\frac{M\left(\frac{1}{2}y-\frac{3}{2}\tau y+\frac{1}{2}\right)}{\delta(\lfloor\tilde{q}_{2}\rfloor)}.

Solving this equation gives

yτ∗:=δ​(⌊q~3⌋−1)2​δ​(⌊q~2⌋)+(3​τ−1)​δ​(⌊q~3⌋−1).y_{\tau}^{*}:=\frac{\delta(\lfloor\tilde{q}_{3}\rfloor-1)}{2\,\delta(\lfloor\tilde{q}_{2}\rfloor)+(3\tau-1)\,\delta(\lfloor\tilde{q}_{3}\rfloor-1)}.

Finally, it follows the non-violatory threshold is then

yτ:=max⁡{yτF,yτ∗}.y_{\tau}:=\max\{y_{\tau}^{F},y_{\tau}^{*}\}.

∎

To summarize the above results, for y∈(yτF,yτ)y\in(y_{\tau}^{F},y_{\tau}), the guaranteed seat of state 1 forces one of the other states below its lower quota, producing a temporary quota violation. This violation disappears once y>yτy>y_{\tau}, consistent with the distribution being ultimately non-violatory. This gives the following:

Corollary 5.19.1.

For distributions that are ultimately non-violatory, if y∈(yτF,yτ)y\in(y_{\tau}^{F},\,y_{\tau}), then the guaranteed seat of state one temporarily causes a quota violation in the apportionment.

Additionally, the preceding results allow a characterization of the structure of xτx_{\tau}:

Theorem 5.20.

(Local structure of xτ)x_{\tau}) The τ\tau-interval (−13,13)(-\frac{1}{3},\frac{1}{3}) can be decomposed into finitely many intervals such that on each interval, xτx_{\tau} is locally represented in the form xτ=ab+c​τ+d​τb+c​τ+ex_{\tau}=\frac{a}{b+c\tau}+\frac{d\tau}{b+c\tau}+e for a,b,c,d,e∈ℝa,b,c,d,e\in\mathbb{R} and denominators not identically 0.

Proof.

By Definition/Lemma 4.7 and Lemma 5.18, yτy_{\tau} is given by the maximum of functions of the form

ab+c​τ\frac{a}{b+c\tau}

when τ\tau is ultimately violatory. For τ\tau ultimately non violatory, the same form holds by Lemma 5.19. Decomposing into intervals for violatory and non-violatory, further decomposing based on which component is the maximum, and recalling x​(y,τ)=12​y−32​τ​y+12x(y,\tau)=\frac{1}{2}y-\frac{3}{2}\tau y+\frac{1}{2} grants the desired result.

∎

Next, look at yy values less than yτFy_{\tau}^{F} and determine the behavior of any possible quota violations.

Lemma 5.21.

If y<yτFy<y_{\tau}^{F}, then no quota violations occur that are caused by the guaranteed seat of state 1 in Huntington-Hill, Modified Jefferson, Dean, and Modified Webster methods.

Proof.

Let AA denote one of the Huntington-Hill, Modified Jefferson, Dean, or Modified Webster methods with divisor function δ\delta. Let the quotas satisfy q1<q2<q3q_{1}<q_{2}<q_{3} and fix τ\tau.

Recall yi​(τ)y_{i}(\tau) from Definition/Lemma 5.17. We may restrict to the case y>y1​(τ)y>y_{1}(\tau) since if y≤y1​(τ)y\leq y_{1}(\tau) q1≥1q_{1}\geq 1 and hence a quota violation caused by the nonzero allocation cannot occur.

The proof proceeds by considering two cases, τ≤0\tau\leq 0 and τ>0\tau>0, due to monotonicity of the quota q2​(y)q_{2}(y) depending on the sign of τ\tau.

Case 1: τ≤0\tau\leq 0. Suppose a quota violation exists. By Lemma 3.6, the floors of the quotas would have the form

(⌊q1⌋,⌊q2⌋,⌊q3⌋)=(0,⌊q~2⌋±s,⌊q~3⌋∓s),s>0.(\lfloor q_{1}\rfloor,\lfloor q_{2}\rfloor,\lfloor q_{3}\rfloor)=(0,\lfloor\tilde{q}_{2}\rfloor\pm s,\lfloor\tilde{q}_{3}\rfloor\mp s),\quad s>0.

But for τ≤0\tau\leq 0, q1q_{1} and q2q_{2} are non-decreasing towards their limits q~2,q~3\tilde{q}_{2},\tilde{q}_{3}. Hence, such floors cannot occur, so no violation exists.

Case 2: τ>0\tau>0. First, note that we may assume y(2,+)≤max⁡(y2,y1)y_{(2,+)}\leq\max(y_{2},y_{1}), as otherwise there would exist yy such that the quotas at yy satisfy (⌊q1⌋,⌊q2⌋,⌊q3⌋)=(0,⌊q~2⌋+s,⌊q3⌋)(\lfloor q_{1}\rfloor,\lfloor q_{2}\rfloor,\lfloor q_{3}\rfloor)=(0,\lfloor\tilde{q}_{2}\rfloor+s,\lfloor q_{3}\rfloor) where s>0s>0. But then ∑⌊qi⌋≥M\sum\lfloor q_{i}\rfloor\geq M and therefore there cannot be a quota violation by Theorem 3.4.
